_note: Major NT→OT citations and allusions with source identification. Curated. Expand as chapters are added. Format: { 'MRK 1:2': { 'type': 'citation'|'allusion', 'source': 'MAL 3:1', 'greek_matches': 'LXX'|'MT'|'paraphrase', 'notes': '...' }}
_source: NA28 marginal references + scholarly consensus (Beale & Carson NT Use of the OT, UBS GNT apparatus).

1TI 2:13.notes: Adam formed first, then Eve — order-of-creation argument.

1TI 5:18.notes: 'ox not muzzled while threshing' matches LXX. Second half ('worker deserves wages') cites Luke 10:7 as 'Scripture' — notable early NT-as-Scripture reference.

1TI 6:7.notes: 'Brought nothing in, take nothing out' echoes Job.

LUK 1:5.notes: Priestly division of Abijah per 1 Chr 24:10; Elizabeth's name shares with Aaron's wife Elisheba (Exod 6:23) — doubly-priestly couple.

LUK 1:6.notes: dikaios-amemptos echoes Noah in Gen 6:9 LXX; 'walking in commandments and statutes' echoes Deuteronomic formula Deut 4:1, 5:1, 6:1.

LUK 1:7.notes: probebekotes en tais hemerais is LXX Gen 18:11 near-verbatim (of Abraham and Sarah). Places Zechariah/Elizabeth in the barren-aged matriarch-pattern including Hannah.

LUK 1:9.notes: Daily incense offering per Exod 30:7-8 — background for Zechariah's priestly service.

LUK 1:10.notes: Gabriel appeared to Daniel 'at the time of the evening sacrifice' (Dan 9:20-21) — tight parallel to Gabriel appearing to Zechariah during incense offering.

LUK 1:13.notes: Pre-natal angelic name-giving parallels Isaac (Gen 17:19) and Samson (Judg 13). Biblical marker of exceptional divine purpose.

LUK 1:14.notes: Joy at miraculous-birth echoes Hannah's exultation at Samuel's birth.

LUK 1:15.notes: oinon kai sikera = Nazirite-vow stipulation from LXX Num 6:3 and especially Judg 13:4 (angel to Samson's mother). John is a Nazirite-from-the-womb, with Jer 1:5 prenatal-prophetic-calling added.

LUK 1:16.notes: 'Turning sons of Israel back to the Lord' echoes Deuteronomic/prophetic repentance-idiom (Deut 30:2, Mal 3:7, Hos 3:5).

LUK 1:17.notes: epistrepsai kardias pateron epi tekna directly quotes Mal 4:6 LXX; 'going before him' echoes Mal 3:1. John is identified as the promised Elijah-return.

LUK 1:18.notes: Zechariah's kata ti gnosomai echoes Abraham's question to YHWH in Gen 15:8 LXX. But Zechariah is rebuked while Abraham was granted covenant-confirmation.

LUK 1:19.notes: Gabriel named only at Dan 8:16 and 9:21 in OT — both end-time revelation contexts. Gabriel's appearing to Daniel 'at the time of the evening sacrifice' is the tight typological parallel.

LUK 1:25.notes: aphelein oneidos mou echoes Gen 30:23 LXX (Rachel at Joseph's birth — apheilen ho theos mou to oneidos). Barrenness-shame reversed, echoing Hannah's pattern.

LUK 1:27.notes: Emphatic parthenos repetition sets up the virginal-conception theology rooted in Isa 7:14.

LUK 1:28.notes: Chaire + 'Lord-is-with-you' formula echoes Zephaniah's daughter-of-Zion-rejoice (Zep 3:14-15) and the Gideon-commissioning-formula (Judg 6:12 LXX). TEXTUAL VARIANT: Byz adds eulogemene su en gynaixin from v.42 assimilation — SBLGNT omits, we follow.

LUK 1:30.notes: heures charin para to theo is LXX idiom for the privileged-elect (Noah Gen 6:8, Moses Exod 33:12-17, David 2 Sam 15:25).

LUK 1:31.notes: sullempse en gastri kai texe hyion directly quotes Isa 7:14 LXX, adapted to 2nd-person.

LUK 1:32.notes: 'Son of the Most High' + 'throne of David his father' directly cites Davidic covenant 2 Sam 7:12-14 LXX. Also echoes Psa 2:7 'You are my Son.'

LUK 1:33.notes: 'His kingdom will have no end' echoes Dan 7:14 LXX (kingdom that shall not be destroyed) + Isa 9:7 LXX (Messianic-peace with no limit) + 2 Sam 7:13 (eternal kingdom to David's son).

LUK 1:35.notes: episkiasei = LXX shekinah-cloud verb (Exod 40:35 of Tabernacle). Holy Spirit + overshadow = creative-divine-presence, also echoed in Luke 9:34 Transfiguration. Son-of-God title 2 Sam 7:14.

LUK 1:37.notes: ouk adunatesei para tou theou pan rhema directly quotes Gen 18:14 LXX — YHWH's rhetorical question to Abraham/Sarah at the impossible-conception announcement.

LUK 1:38.notes: doule + humble-servant language anticipates Magnificat's fuller quotation of 1 Sam 1:11 (Hannah's vow).

LUK 1:41.notes: eskirtesen en te koilia echoes Gen 25:22 LXX (Jacob-and-Esau in-utero-leaping as prophetic sign).

LUK 1:42.notes: eulogemene su en gynaixin echoes Judg 5:24 LXX (Deborah's blessing on Jael — enemy-breaker typology). 'Fruit of your womb' echoes Deuteronomic blessing Deut 28:4.

LUK 1:43.notes: Elizabeth's 'why should the mother of my Lord come to me?' echoes David's 'how shall the ark of the Lord come to me?' (2 Sam 6:9) — Mary-as-new-Ark typology.

LUK 1:45.notes: 'Blessed is she who believed' places Mary in Abrahamic-faith-paradigm (Gen 15:6) and Psalm-beatitude-formula (Ps 1:1).

LUK 1:46.notes: Magnificat opening — directly echoes 1 Sam 2:1 LXX (Hannah's song) + Ps 34:3 LXX (megalynate ton kurion).

LUK 1:47.notes: egalliasen to pneuma mou epi to theo to soteri mou near-verbatim Hab 3:18 LXX. Salvation-joy theme from Hannah.

LUK 1:48.notes: epeblepsen epi ten tapeinosin tes doules autou verbatim Hannah's vow 1 Sam 1:11 LXX.

LUK 1:49.notes: epoiesen megala echoes Deut 10:21; hagion to onoma autou echoes Ps 111:9 LXX.

LUK 1:50.notes: to eleos autou...tois phoboumenois auton quotes Ps 103:17 LXX (LXX Ps 102:17).

LUK 1:53.notes: peinontas eneplesen agathon verbatim Ps 107:9 LXX.

LUK 1:54.notes: Israel-servant + remembered-mercy echoes Isa 41:8-10 LXX and Ps 98:3 LXX.

LUK 1:55.notes: Promise to Abraham and his seed forever = Mic 7:20 + Gen 17:7 + Gen 22:17-18 (Abrahamic oath).

LUK 1:68.notes: Eulogetos kyrios ho theos tou Israel is standard psalmic-benediction-formula (Ps 41:13, 72:18, 106:48 LXX). epeskepsato = LXX redemptive-visiting verb (Gen 50:24, Exod 3:16, Ruth 1:6). lytrosis = Exodus-redemption imagery.

LUK 1:69.notes: keras soterias en oiko Dauid directly cites Ps 132:17 LXX (horn-sprouting for David). Also echoes Ps 18:2 (horn of my salvation) and 1 Sam 2:10 (horn of his anointed).

LUK 1:71.notes: ek cheiros...misounton hemas quotes Ps 106:10 LXX (ek cheiros misountos).

LUK 1:72.notes: mnesthenai diathekes echoes Ps 106:45 LXX (God-remembered-covenant) and broader Exod 2:24 tradition.

LUK 1:73.notes: horkon hon omosen directly references Gen 22:16 LXX (Abrahamic oath after Akedah).

LUK 1:76.notes: hetoimasai hodous autou directly cites Isa 40:3 LXX; proporeuse enopion kyriou echoes Mal 3:1 LXX (messenger-before-the-Lord).

LUK 1:77.notes: Knowledge-of-salvation-through-forgiveness echoes new-covenant theme Jer 31:34 and Isaianic-servant atonement Isa 53:11.

LUK 1:78.notes: anatole ex hypsous = Messianic polysemic 'dawn/rising-star/shoot.' Direct citations: Mal 4:2 LXX (sun of righteousness rises); Isa 9:2 LXX (light in darkness). Thematic: Num 24:17 LXX (star-from-Jacob), Jer 23:5, Zec 3:8 (anatole as Messianic-title, 'the Branch').

LUK 1:79.notes: tois en skotei kai skia thanatou kathemenois near-verbatim Ps 107:10 LXX. 'Path of peace' echoes Isa 59:8 LXX. Shadow-of-death echoes Ps 23:4 LXX.

LUK 1:80.notes: John's wilderness-dwelling continues Elijah-typology (1 Kgs 19 Elijah in wilderness).

LUK 2:4.notes: Joseph's Davidic descent + Bethlehem is not cited explicitly; Luke trusts the informed reader to connect with the Messianic birth prophecy.

LUK 2:7.notes: firstborn (prōtotokos) motif anticipates the explicit firstborn-consecration at vv.22-23.

LUK 2:8.notes: Migdal Eder (Tower of the Flock) Messianic-shepherd-locale; shepherds near Bethlehem tending Temple flocks is Jewish tradition consistent with this.

LUK 2:9.notes: doxa kyriou = kavod YHWH — theophanic language: Exod 40 tabernacle, Isa 60 returning light. Luke signals incarnational theophany.

LUK 2:11.notes: Davidic covenant — messiah from David's line; 'city of David' locator reinforces. Psalm 2:2 'Lord's Anointed' also in view.

LUK 2:13.notes: stratia ourania = 'host of heaven' = tzva hashamayim; 1 Kgs 22:19 Micaiah's vision, Ps 103:21 LXX angelic praise. Ironic reversal: heavenly army announces peace, not war.

LUK 2:14.notes: Messianic peace + cosmic reconciliation themes. Also textual-variant context (eudokias vs eudokia) — following SBLGNT genitive = peace to men of divine favor.

LUK 2:21.notes: 8th-day circumcision per Abrahamic covenant (Gen 17:12) and Mosaic code (Lev 12:3).

LUK 2:22.notes: Post-partum purification (Lev 12) + firstborn-presentation (Exod 13:2) compressed in one Jerusalem trip.

LUK 2:23.notes: Luke's pan arsen dianoigon mētran hagion tō kyriō klēthēsetai is a free summary of Exod 13:2 + 13:12 + 13:15 LXX. Passover-firstborn theology frame.

LUK 2:24.notes: zeugos trygonōn ē dyo nossous peristerōn directly quotes LXX Lev 12:8 (poor-mother alternative offering). Signals Joseph-Mary's poverty.

LUK 2:25.notes: 'Consolation of Israel' (paraklēsis tou Israēl) = Messianic technical expression tied to Isaianic consolation-of-Zion texts.

LUK 2:26.notes: 'the Lord's Christ' = mashiach YHWH — royal-Davidic Messianic title from Psalm 2:2 and 1 Samuel's Davidic anointing passages.

LUK 2:29.notes: Simeon's 'depart in peace' (apolyeis ton doulon sou en eirēnē) echoes LXX Gen 15:15 where God tells Abraham he will 'go to your fathers with peace' (apeleusē pros tous pateras sou met' eirēnēs). Faithful-servant-departing-in-peace motif.

LUK 2:30.notes: eidon hoi ophthalmoi mou to sōtērion sou echoes Isa 40:5 LXX ('all flesh shall see the salvation of God') and Isa 52:10 ('all the earth shall see the salvation'). Simeon sees what Isaiah prophesied all flesh would see.

LUK 2:31.notes: 'prepared before all peoples' echoes Psa 98:2-3 LXX where God's salvation is made known to all nations.

LUK 2:32.notes: phōs eis apokalypsin ethnōn kai doxan laou sou Israēl compresses Isaiah's Servant Songs: Isa 42:6 + Isa 49:6 ('light of nations') + Isa 46:13 ('glory of Israel'). Gentile inclusion programmatic for Luke-Acts.

LUK 2:34.notes: 'fall and rise' echoes Isa 8:14-15 (stone of stumbling) + Isa 28:16 (cornerstone). Jesus as the dividing sign who reveals Israel's orientation to God.

LUK 2:36.notes: Paired male-female witness (Simeon + Anna) fulfills Deut 19:15's two-witness requirement for legal testimony.

LUK 2:37.notes: Anna (Hebrew Hannah) + temple-devotion + widow-piety motifs parallel Hannah in 1 Sam 1. Namesake connection reinforces the infancy-narrative's Samuel typology.

LUK 2:38.notes: 'redemption of Jerusalem' (lytrōsis Ierousalēm) directly echoes Isa 52:9 'the Lord has comforted his people, he has redeemed Jerusalem' (parakaleō + lytroō paired).

LUK 2:40.notes: Child grew + became strong + wisdom + grace-of-God closely echoes 1 Sam 2:21, 2:26 LXX of Samuel. Deliberate Samuel-typology.

LUK 2:41.notes: Annual Passover pilgrimage per Deut 16:16 three-festivals requirement; Exod 12:14 institution.

LUK 2:47.notes: Boy Jesus's understanding (synesis) + wise answers echo Messianic Isa 11:2 'spirit of wisdom and understanding' resting on the Messiah.

LUK 2:49.notes: Jesus's 'my Father' (tou patros mou) at his first recorded words signals unique Sonship awareness — Ps 2:7 'you are my son, today I have begotten you' thematically underlies, though not directly quoted.

LUK 2:52.notes: Jesus grew in wisdom + stature + favor with God and men directly parallels 1 Sam 2:26 LXX of Samuel; Prov 3:4 'find favor + understanding before God and people' also in view.

LUK 3:1.notes: Prophetic-call chronological-formula: extended dating by multiple rulers echoes OT prophetic-call conventions (Isaiah 6:1 Uzziah; Jeremiah 1:1-3 Josiah-Jehoiakim-Zedekiah; Ezekiel 1:1-3 Jehoiachin-exile year). Luke 3:1-2 seven-ruler catalog launches narrative in classical prophetic style.

LUK 3:2.notes: Classic prophetic-call formula ἐγένετο ῥῆμα θεοῦ ἐπί- = Heb. wayəhî dəḇar-YHWH ʾel-. Direct LXX equivalent of OT prophetic-word-came-to-X pattern. Marks the end of 400-year prophetic silence since Malachi.

LUK 3:4.notes: ISA 40:3 LXX verbatim except αὐτοῦ for LXX τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν — same modification Mark and Matthew make. Synoptic-parallel verbatim with MRK 1:3 + MAT 3:3. Luke is unique in extending the Isaiah quote through 40:5 (vv.5-6).

LUK 3:5.notes: ISA 40:4 LXX with minor Lukan paraphrase (drops πάντα, shifts τραχεῖα-sing to plural, writes ὁδοὺς λείας for LXX πεδία). Matthew and Mark do not include this verse — Lukan universalism extends the quote.

LUK 3:6.notes: ISA 40:5 LXX final-clause citation (drops first clause about YHWH glory appearing). UNIQUE TO LUKE. Universal-salvation scope (πᾶσα σάρξ = all humanity/all flesh) is Lukan theological signature (cf. 2:32; Acts 1:8). Closes inclusio with Simeon σωτήριον at 2:30.

LUK 3:7.notes: The coming-wrath (μέλλουσα ὀργή) language echoes OT day-of-YHWH prophetic tradition. Synoptic parallel MAT 3:7.

LUK 3:8.notes: Abrahamic-covenant background (Gen 17:4-7 father of many nations) which crowds presume on. ISA 51:1-2 rock-from-which-you-were-hewn invokes the same stone/sons (banim/abanim) Hebrew wordplay that stands behind the saying.

LUK 3:11.notes: Share-with-those-in-need ethic of Torah poverty-law: Deut 15:7-11 (open hand to poor brother) + Lev 19:9-10 (gleaning-leave for poor+sojourner). John's concrete ethic of repentance = obedience to Torah neighbor-love. Anticipates early-church community-of-goods (Acts 2:44-45; 4:34-35).

LUK 3:13.notes: Just-weights/honest-measures ethic: Lev 19:35-36 prohibits dishonest weights; Mic 6:11 condemns deceitful scales; Pro 11:1 false-balance-abomination. John rebukes tax-collector extortion within the same prophetic-moral tradition.

LUK 3:14.notes: Just-war ethics (Deut 20) + contentment theology (Prov 30:7-9) converge in John the Baptist's instruction to soldiers. Unique-to-Luke material.

LUK 3:15.notes: Elijah-forerunner expectation (Mal 4:5-6) fuels the crowd's speculation whether John is the Messiah. Unique-to-Luke.

LUK 3:16.notes: Baptism-with-Spirit-and-fire: ISA 4:4 spirit-of-judgment/burning; MAL 3:2-3 refiner's fire; JOL 2:28-29 Spirit outpoured (explicitly fulfilled ACT 2:16-18). Dual-destination polysemy preserves both refining-purification and eschatological-judgment readings. Synoptic parallel MAT 3:11.

LUK 3:17.notes: Unquenchable-fire (πυρὶ ἀσβέστῳ) = ISA 66:24 eschatological-judgment language (also cited at MRK 9:43-48). Threshing-floor judgment image from MIC 4:12. Day-burning-as-furnace MAL 4:1. Synoptic parallel MAT 3:12.

LUK 3:19.notes: Herodias-marriage violates Lev 18:16 + 20:21 (brother's-wife prohibition, applicable since brother was still living — no levirate exception per Deut 25:5-10).

LUK 3:21.notes: Heavens-opened theophany: ISA 64:1 (Oh, that you would tear open the heavens and come down) + EZK 1:1 (the heavens were opened, I saw visions of God). Luke uses plainer ἀνοίγω vs. Mark's more-dramatic σχίζω.

LUK 3:22.notes: Father's voice composite: (a) "you are my Son" = PSA 2:7 royal-Messianic; (b) "the beloved" = GEN 22:2 Isaac-allusion (only/beloved son offered); (c) "in you I am well pleased" = ISA 42:1 servant-song. Triadic christological-theological claim: King + Sacrificial-Son + Suffering-Servant. Identical composite to MAT 3:17 + MRK 1:11.

LUK 3:23.notes: Age-30 biblical-significance: priestly-service age (Num 4:3), David reign-start age (2 Sam 5:4), Joseph-in-Egypt age (Gen 41:46). Jesus begins ministry at the age-threshold of priestly/royal/patriarchal service.

LUK 3:27.notes: Zerubbabel and Shealtiel are named post-exilic leaders (Ezra 3:2 Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel led temple-rebuilding; Neh 12:1 exile-return list).

LUK 3:31.notes: Nathan-son-of-David: 2 Sam 5:14 + 1 Chr 3:5 list David's sons born in Jerusalem, including Nathan. Luke's genealogy diverges from Matthew at this point: Matthew traces Joseph through Solomon (MAT 1:6-7); Luke traces through Nathan. Evangelical harmonization: Luke = Mary's biological line (avoiding Jeconiah curse per JER 22:30); Matthew = Joseph's legal-royal line.

LUK 3:32.notes: Perez-to-David genealogy matches Ruth 4:18-22 (book of Ruth's closing genealogy) + 1 Chr 2:10-15 (parallel Davidic descent list). Luke rejoins Matthew's genealogy here; matching David-to-Abraham segment.

LUK 3:33.notes: Perez-Hezron-Amminadab lineage per Ruth 4:18-19 + origin-of-Perez at Gen 38:29 (Judah+Tamar). Luke's SBLGNT reading has Ἀδμίν + Ἀρνί (two names) — evangelical critical-text reading — where Matthew/MT Ruth has "Aram" (one name). Minor textual variant; Luke has one extra generation.

LUK 3:34.notes: Abraham-Terah-Nahor lineage per Gen 11:24-26. Luke continues past Abraham (where Matthew begins) to universalize Jesus's genealogy.

LUK 3:35.notes: Post-flood Semitic genealogy: Serug-Reu-Peleg-Eber-Shelah per Gen 11:10-26 (Heb. and LXX agree here). Peleg-division name-meaning per Gen 10:25.

LUK 3:36.notes: LXX-vs-MT textual divergence at Cainan: Luke's genealogy includes Καϊνάμ (Cainan) as Arphaxad's son — present in LXX Gen 11:12 but ABSENT from Masoretic Hebrew. Luke follows LXX. Not a harmonization crisis; Luke regularly uses LXX. Lamech-Noah also per Gen 5:28-29.

LUK 3:37.notes: Pre-flood genealogy from Cainan (son of Enosh) to Methuselah per Gen 5:9-27. Enoch (Ἑνώχ) theologically significant: Gen 5:24 walked-with-God + taken; Heb 11:5 + Jude 14 NT commentary.

LUK 3:38.notes: Adam-Seth-Enosh per Gen 4:25 + 5:1-6. Luke's distinctive termination: τοῦ θεοῦ (of God) — Adam as God's son by creation (imago Dei per Gen 1:26-27). Universalizes Jesus's lineage to all humanity. Sets up Adam-Christ typology developed in ROM 5:14 + 1CO 15:22, 45.

LUK 4:2.notes: 40-day fast paradigm (Moses on Sinai; Elijah to Horeb) + Israel's 40-year wilderness testing typology — Jesus as true Israel succeeds where the nation failed at hunger-testing.

LUK 4:3.notes: Wilderness hunger-testing framework: as Yahweh tested Israel with hunger to know what was in their heart, so devil tests the Son with hunger to see if he will trust.

LUK 4:4.notes: οὐκ ἐπ' ἄρτῳ μόνῳ ζήσεται ὁ ἄνθρωπος verbatim from LXX Deut 8:3 first clause. SBLGNT/NA28/BSB end here; Byzantine adds the Matthean continuation 'ἀλλ' ἐπὶ παντὶ ῥήματι θεοῦ' as scribal harmonization. We follow SBLGNT (no bracket — the half-citation is the authentic Lukan text).

LUK 4:8.notes: κύριον τὸν θεόν σου προσκυνήσεις καὶ αὐτῷ μόνῳ λατρεύσεις. LXX Deut 6:13 has φοβηθήσῃ for MT תירא 'fear'; Jesus/Lukan tradition uses προσκυνήσεις 'worship' (perhaps via Targumic-influenced reading). αὐτῷ μόνῳ 'him only' is also a Jesus-emphasis addition not strictly in MT/LXX. Part of the Shema-cluster (Deut 6:4-15). Synoptic parallel Matt 4:10.

LUK 4:10.notes: Devil quotes LXX Ps 90:11 (MT Ps 91:11): τοῖς ἀγγέλοις αὐτοῦ ἐντελεῖται περὶ σοῦ τοῦ διαφυλάξαι σε. DELIBERATELY SELECTIVE: omits «ἐν πάσαις ταῖς ὁδοῖς σου» (in all your ways) which qualifies divine protection as covering the faithful believer's ordinary paths, not demand-test scenarios. HAPAX: διαφυλάσσω.

LUK 4:11.notes: Continuation of devil's Ps 91 quote: ἐπὶ χειρῶν ἀροῦσίν σε μήποτε προσκόψῃς πρὸς λίθον τὸν πόδα σου verbatim from LXX Ps 90:12. Even truncated, the citation is rhetorically skilled — Ps 91 is genuinely a messianic-protection psalm.

LUK 4:12.notes: οὐκ ἐκπειράσεις κύριον τὸν θεόν σου verbatim from LXX Deut 6:16. Original Deut context references Massah (Ex 17:1-7) where Israel demanded water-proof from Yahweh. Jesus refuses the parallel demand for self-protection-proof. All three temptation-responses (vv.4, 8, 12) come from Deut 6-8 (Shema-cluster) — Jesus as true Israel rehearses the Torah Israel failed to keep.

LUK 4:18.notes: Composite quotation of LXX Isa 61:1-2a + LXX Isa 58:6d. Jesus combines two Jubilee-release texts sharing ἄφεσις/ἀποστέλλω vocabulary. Three HAPAX triggered by LXX-source: αἰχμάλωτος, ἀνάβλεψις, θραύω. The omitted phrase from Isa 61:1 'to bind up the brokenhearted' (LXX ἰάσασθαι τοὺς συντετριμμένους τὴν καρδίαν) — scholars debate whether Jesus or Lukan-tradition skipped. LXX form (not MT) shows Lukan source-dependency. Programmatic: Jesus identifies himself as the Anointed One of Isa 61.

LUK 4:19.notes: κηρύξαι ἐνιαυτὸν κυρίου δεκτόν from LXX Isa 61:2a. KEY THEOLOGICAL TRUNCATION: stops mid-verse, omitting Isa 61:2b «καὶ ἡμέραν ἀνταποδόσεως» ('and the day of vengeance'). Deliberate cut signals Jesus's first-advent program as proclamation-of-grace, deferring final-judgment to future eschaton. δεκτόν echoes back to v.24 (no prophet δεκτός in his hometown) — verbal play between accepted-year and rejected-prophet.

LUK 4:25.notes: Reference to Elijah's drought announcement (1 Kgs 17:1) and the 3.5-year duration. Strict 1 Kings 18:1 says 'in the third year' — Jewish tradition (Sirach 48:3, b. Yebamoth 121b) and Jas 5:17 give 3.5 years, which Luke follows. The drought provides setting for Jesus's Gentile-grace illustration.

LUK 4:26.notes: Reference to Elijah and the widow of Zarephath in Sidon (1 Kgs 17:8-24). HAPAX: Σάρεπτα. Theological scandal — God bypasses Israelite need to provide for a Gentile widow. Foreshadows Lukan Gentile-mission theme (cf. 2:32; Acts).

LUK 4:27.notes: Reference to Naaman the Syrian's cleansing through Elisha (2 Kgs 5:1-14). THREE HAPAX (proper-noun): Ἐλισαῖος, Ναιμάν, Σύρος. Escalation from v.26 — not just foreign widow (passive recipient) but foreign army-commander (active enemy). Both Elijah and Elisha episodes ground the Gentile-grace theme that enrages the Nazareth audience in v.28.

LUK 5:8.notes: Peter's self-identification as a sinful man in response to theophany parallels Isaiah's 'I am a man of unclean lips' response to the temple vision. Same pattern: sinful human encounters divine holiness and recognizes own unworthiness.

LUK 5:12.notes: Leper's condition 'full of leprosy' recalls the diagnostic categories of Lev 13 (leprosy purity code). His approach to Jesus violates Levitical purity separation rules.

LUK 5:13.notes: Jesus's touch inverts the Levitical rule that contact with uncleanness transfers pollution. Here cleansing flows outward from Jesus to the leper, not pollution inward.

LUK 5:14.notes: Jesus instructs the healed leper to present the offering Moses prescribed for cleansing — direct reference to the Levitical ritual of restoration (lamb or doves per economic status).

LUK 5:20.notes: Jesus's pronouncement of forgiveness invokes YHWH's self-declaration in Isaiah that He alone blots out transgressions. The scribes rightly recognize this as a divine prerogative (v.21).

LUK 5:21.notes: Scribes' objection 'who can forgive sins but God alone' echoes ISA 43:25's exclusivity — YHWH alone forgives sin. Their theology is correct; their recognition is incomplete.

LUK 5:24.notes: First 'Son of Man' saying in Luke. Title derives from Daniel 7:13-14 where one 'like a son of man' receives eternal dominion from the Ancient of Days. Jesus claims divine authority to forgive sins on earth via this Danielic title.

LUK 5:30.notes: Pharisees' grumbling (ἐγόγγυζον) echoes the LXX vocabulary of Israel's wilderness grumbling against Moses and YHWH. The verb γογγύζω in LXX almost exclusively appears in the wilderness-rebellion narratives (Exo 16; Num 11, 14, 16, 17). Luke subtly positions the Pharisees as successors to Israel's rebellious tendency.

LUK 5:34.notes: Jesus's self-identification as the bridegroom invokes the OT marriage metaphor for YHWH and Israel. By taking this title, Jesus implicitly claims the divine role in the covenant-marriage. Cf. JHN 3:29 where John the Baptist calls Jesus the bridegroom.

LUK 6:3.notes: Jesus's appeal to the David-and-showbread narrative (1 Sam 21:1-6) to justify his disciples' Sabbath-plucking. David, fleeing Saul, received consecrated showbread from priest Ahimelech at Nob. Jesus uses this as a greater-to-lesser argument: if the anointed-king-in-need could eat priest-only bread, the greater-than-David Son-of-Man can grant Sabbath-flexibility. The 'have you not read' formula is Jesus's signature Scripture-appeal-against-religious-leaders (cf. MAT 12:3, 19:4, 21:16, 21:42, 22:31).

LUK 6:4.notes: Luke 6:4 continues the David-showbread narrative (1 Sam 21:6 the specific act of eating) and cross-references the Levitical law (Lev 24:5-9) which restricted the bread-of-the-presence to the Aaronic priests as a perpetual-due. Jesus's argument: David's act was technically a ceremonial-transgression, but Ahimelech granted it and David received it without divine-censure, showing that ritual-law yields to the greater-claim of God's-anointed-servant in need.

LUK 6:13.notes: The choice of twelve apostles echoes the twelve-tribe structure of Israel — Jesus explicitly frames his movement as new-Israel-foundation. Cf. LUK 22:30 ('you will sit on thrones judging the twelve tribes'), REV 21:12-14 (twelve gates + twelve foundations of new Jerusalem). The number is not incidental but theologically-loaded.

LUK 6:17.notes: The crowd from 'Tyre and Sidon' (Gentile Phoenician cities) alongside the Judean Jews is a subtle Lukan-universality marker. Luke's gospel has the Isaiah 49:6 'light-to-the-Gentiles' theme running from Simeon's song (LUK 2:32) through Acts (ACT 13:47). Gentile-inclusion is Luke's distinctive theological emphasis.

LUK 6:20.notes: 'Blessed are the poor' echoes Isaiah 61:1's 'good news to the afflicted/poor' (LXX: εὐαγγελίσασθαι πτωχοῖς) — the very text Jesus read at his Nazareth-synagogue inauguration (LUK 4:18-19). Luke's literal 'poor' (πτωχοί) preserves the social-justice edge that Isaiah 61 originally carried. The kingdom-reversal: the materially-poor, the people Isaiah's servant was sent to, now receive the kingdom.

LUK 6:21.notes: Isaiah 61:3 promises 'comfort to those who mourn...garments of praise for the spirit of heaviness' — Jesus's hungry-now-filled and weeping-now-laughing extend this promise. Psalm 126:5-6 'those who sow in tears shall reap in joy' provides the eschatological-reversal pattern. Both texts ground the beatitudes in OT kingdom-hope.

LUK 6:23.notes: 'Their fathers treated the prophets in the same way' explicitly invokes the persecuted-prophets tradition of the OT. 2 Chronicles 36:16 summarizes the pattern: 'they mocked God's messengers...until there was no remedy.' Stephen's speech (ACT 7:52) makes this explicit: 'which of the prophets did your fathers not persecute?' The persecution-of-prophets is a theological-diagnostic of covenant-unfaithfulness.

LUK 6:24.notes: The οὐαί-formula follows OT-prophetic-pattern (Isaiah 5:8-23 has six woes; Amos 5:18-20, 6:1; Habakkuk 2:6-19). Amos 6:1 'woe to those at ease in Zion' is a close parallel — pronouncing judgment on the wealthy-complacent. Isaiah 5:8 pronounces woe on those who 'join house to house' (accumulation-without-end). Jesus extends this prophetic-woe-form to his own audience.

LUK 6:26.notes: Micah 2:11 satirizes the popular false-prophet: 'If a man...should preach to you of wine and strong drink, he would be this people's preacher.' Jeremiah 23:16-17 denounces prophets who 'keep saying to those who despise me, the LORD has said, It shall be well with you.' These texts provide the OT-pattern for Jesus's warning that universal-praise is the mark of false-prophecy, not true-prophecy.

LUK 6:27.notes: Jesus radicalizes Leviticus 19:18's 'love your neighbor' by extending 'neighbor' to include 'enemy.' Proverbs 25:21-22 provides the practical-behavior model: 'If your enemy is hungry, give him bread to eat; if he is thirsty, give him water to drink.' Paul quotes PRO 25:21-22 in Rom 12:20. Jesus's love-enemies teaching is the fulfillment-extension of Torah's neighbor-love.

LUK 6:30.notes: Deuteronomy 15:7-11 commands Israel to 'open wide your hand' to the poor and 'lend him sufficient for his need.' Jesus extends this to radical-generosity: give to everyone who asks, don't demand back what's taken. Torah's generous-lending ethic becomes Jesus's non-reciprocal-giving ethic.

LUK 6:35.notes: Psalm 145:9 'The LORD is good to all, and his mercy is over all that he has made.' God's universal-kindness toward-all-creation (including the ungrateful and wicked) grounds the disciple's enemy-love ethic. Jesus's logic: imitate God's universal-kindness-to-all by extending kindness-to-enemies.

LUK 6:36.notes: Luke's 'be merciful as your Father is merciful' is the Lukan-form of the holiness-imitation imperative of Leviticus 19:2 'be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy.' The mercy-facet comes from Exodus 34:6 where God self-names YHWH YHWH, a God merciful (רַחוּם rahum / οἰκτίρμων) and gracious. Matthew 5:48 ('be perfect as your heavenly Father is perfect') uses the holiness/completeness facet; Luke uses the mercy facet. Same imitatio-dei structure, different divine-attribute emphasized.

LUK 6:37.notes: Leviticus 19:15-18 commands righteous-judgment-without-partiality plus neighbor-love. Jesus extends to 'do-not-judge-at-all' (in the sense of condemnatory-judgment). The mishnaic measure-for-measure principle (Sotah 1:7) captures the same reciprocity-logic Jesus uses here. Rabbi Hillel's near-contemporary teaching paralleled this.

LUK 6:41.notes: Psalm 19:12-13 'Who can discern his errors? Cleanse me from hidden faults. Keep back your servant also from presumptuous sins.' The Psalmist's self-examination-prayer models the inward-first-discernment Jesus commends. The rabbinic parallel (b. Arachin 16b, Rabbi Tarphon) also captures the cultural critique of self-righteous-reproof.

LUK 6:45.notes: Proverbs 4:23 'Guard your heart with all diligence, for from it flow the springs of life.' Proverbs 16:23 'The heart of the wise makes his speech judicious.' Both texts establish the heart-speech connection Jesus uses: what is inside the heart inevitably comes out in speech and action. The heart as the inner-source of outward-behavior is a core wisdom-theology theme.

LUK 6:46.notes: Malachi 1:6 'A son honors his father, and a servant his master. If then I am a father, where is my honor? If I am a master, where is my fear?' God's critique of empty-ritual-honor without substantive-obedience matches Jesus's rebuke of 'Lord, Lord' without obedience. Both texts critique the gap between verbal-confession and actual-loyalty.

LUK 6:48.notes: Isaiah 28:16 'Behold, I am the one who has laid as a foundation in Zion, a stone, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation' establishes the firm-foundation motif Jesus uses. Isaiah 28:17-19 contrasts this with the 'overflowing scourge' that sweeps away the refuge-of-lies. Proverbs 10:25 'When the whirlwind passes, the wicked is no more, but the righteous has an everlasting foundation' provides the whirlwind-reveals-foundation image.

LUK 6:49.notes: The collapse-of-the-foundationless-house pairs with v.48 on the same Isaiah-28 / Proverbs-10 foundation-theology. The 'great ruin' (ῥῆγμα μέγα) is the decisive-judgment image — those who hear but do not obey face catastrophic-collapse when the flood comes. Proverbs 10:25 specifically contrasts the wicked (swept-away) with the righteous (everlasting-foundation).

LUK 7:11.notes: Nain is geographically near Shunem where Elisha raised the widow's son (2 Kgs 4:18-37); the widow-and-only-son motif also echoes 1 Kgs 17:17-24 (Elijah and widow of Zarephath). Luke places this miracle here to frame Jesus as new-Elijah/Elisha — a Lukan programmatic concern established at 1:17 (John in spirit-of-Elijah) and 4:25-27 (Jesus's Nazareth-sermon citing Elijah+Elisha).

LUK 7:13.notes: ἐσπλαγχνίσθη is the Greek-lexicalization of Hebrew rachamim (רַחֲמִים = womb-compassion). In OT this is YHWH's own compassion (Ps 103:13 'as a father has compassion on his children, so the LORD has compassion on those who fear him'; Isa 49:15 mother-not-forgetting-nursing-child). Luke's first narratorial ὁ κύριος + σπλαγχνίζομαι marks Jesus as the compassion-of-YHWH embodied.

LUK 7:15.notes: καὶ ἔδωκεν αὐτὸν τῇ μητρὶ αὐτοῦ = VERBATIM LXX 1 Kgs 17:23 (Elijah returns widow's son to his mother at Zarephath). Luke's lexical-signature: explicit intertextual echo of Elijah's greatest miracle, now surpassed (Jesus commands vs Elijah prays + stretches three times). Completes the new-Elijah frame begun at v.11.

LUK 7:16.notes: ἐπεσκέψατο ὁ θεὸς τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ matches LXX-formula for YHWH's saving-visitation: Gen 21:1 (visiting Sarah to give Isaac), Exo 3:16 (visiting Israel in Egypt), Ruth 1:6 (visiting Naomi's people with bread). Lukan inclusio with 1:68 εὐλογητὸς κύριος ... ἐπεσκέψατο (Benedictus) and 1:78 ἐπισκέψεται. Crowd's language is theologically precise even if their Christology stops at 'great-prophet.'

LUK 7:22.notes: SHARED WITH MAT 11:5. Six-item Messianic-signs-list: blind-see / lame-walk / lepers-cleansed / deaf-hear / dead-raised / poor-evangelized. Items 1,2,4 match ISA 35:5-6 LXX near-verbatim (τυφλοί + χωλοί + κωφοί); item 6 (πτωχοὶ εὐαγγελίζονται) matches ISA 61:1 LXX verbatim (εὐαγγελίσασθαι πτωχοῖς). Items 3 (lepers) and 5 (dead-raised) EXCEED Isaiah's prophecy — Jesus's ministry-signs surpass even what was promised. Jesus's answer to John IS the self-evident Messianic-claim: what Isaiah foretold is happening + more.

LUK 7:27.notes: SHARED WITH MAT 11:10 / MRK 1:2. MAJOR COMPOSITE CITATION. Opening phrase ἰδοὺ ἀποστέλλω τὸν ἄγγελόν μου πρὸ προσώπου σου = VERBATIM EXO 23:20 LXX (YHWH sends angel ahead of Israel into Canaan). Continuation ὃς κατασκευάσει τὴν ὁδόν σου ἔμπροσθέν σου adapts MAL 3:1 LXX (ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἐξαποστέλλω τὸν ἄγγελόν μου, καὶ ἐπιβλέψεται ὁδὸν πρὸ προσώπου μου). CHRISTOLOGICAL HIGH-WATERMARK: Jesus/Luke change Malachi's 'ahead-of-me' (πρὸ προσώπου μου) to 'ahead-of-you' (πρὸ προσώπου σου) — reassigning YHWH's forerunner to now-go-before Messiah. Shared pre-Synoptic tradition.

LUK 7:28.notes: γεννητοὶ γυναικῶν = Hebraism 'those born of women' for humans-generally (cf. Job 14:1 'Man who is born of a woman'; Job 15:14; Job 25:4). Jesus uses the OT-idiom to frame a superlative: of all humans up to this point, no one is greater than John. Sets up the epochal-distinction of kingdom-of-God surpassing all pre-kingdom distinctions.

LUK 7:34.notes: Pharisees' accusation ἄνθρωπος φάγος καὶ οἰνοπότης echoes DEU 21:20 LXX rebellious-son formula συμβολοκοπῶν οἰνοφλυγεῖ ('glutton, drunkard'), which in Torah warrants stoning. The Pharisees deploy a serious Torah-category against Jesus, not casual insult. Jesus's retort in v.35 subverts: wisdom is vindicated by its children, not its Torah-wielding accusers.

LUK 7:35.notes: Lady-Wisdom personification-tradition: 'Children' of Wisdom = those who embrace her (Prov 8:32 'Blessed are those who keep my ways'). The vindication-of-Wisdom-by-children formula echoes Sir 4:11 (Wisdom's-children) as well. Jesus applies the wisdom-tradition to the John-Jesus reception: those responding-in-faith prove God's two-fold emissary-strategy right.

LUK 7:50.notes: πορεύου εἰς εἰρήνην = LXX-formula for farewell-blessing (לֵךְ לְשָׁלוֹם). Eli blesses Hannah (1 Sam 1:17), Jonathan blesses David (1 Sam 20:42), priest blesses Danites (Judg 18:6). Jesus's closing-pronouncement draws on the OT-farewell-blessing tradition. This is the FIRST LUK use (will repeat at 8:48 to hemorrhaging woman).

LUK 8:10.notes: Luke's abbreviated form of Isaiah 6:9 LXX. LXX reads: Ἀκοῇ ἀκούσετε καὶ οὐ μὴ συνῆτε, καὶ βλέποντες βλέψετε καὶ οὐ μὴ ἴδητε. Luke compresses to ἵνα βλέποντες μὴ βλέπωσιν καὶ ἀκούοντες μὴ συνιῶσιν (same-root repetition preserved — seeing-see, hearing-understand). Luke's version is markedly shorter than Matt 13:14-15 (full LXX quote) and Mark 4:12 (Targum-influenced). Luke's ἵνα clause-structure shifts from Isaiah's indicative-prophetic-command to explicit-purpose: Jesus speaks in parables SO THAT the non-disciples will encounter the same hardening-judgment Isaiah announced. Applied to Jesus's parabolic teaching as prophetic-fulfillment pattern. Parallels: Mark 4:12, Matt 13:13-15, John 12:40, Acts 28:26-27.

LUK 9:14.notes: Grouping of the 5000 into κλισίας ... ἀνὰ πεντήκοντα (groups of fifty) echoes Moses organizing Israel into thousands-hundreds-fifties-tens (Exo 18:21,25; Deut 1:15). Frames the feeding as a new-wilderness-feeding with Jesus as the new-Moses.

LUK 9:17.notes: Feeding-miracle with leftovers echoes Elisha multiplying bread for 100 men in 2 Kings 4:42-44 (τὸ περισσεῦσαν αὐτοῖς κλασμάτων). Also the wilderness-manna (Exo 16).

LUK 9:22.notes: The "third day" resurrection-formula (τῇ τρίτῃ ἡμέρᾳ ἐγερθῆναι) alludes to Hosea 6:2 LXX (ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ ἀναστησόμεθα). Jonah 1:17 (three days-three nights) is an alternate source.

LUK 9:26.notes: Son-of-Man coming in glory (ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ... ἐν τῇ δόξῃ αὐτοῦ) directly alludes to Daniel 7:13-14 LXX vision of one-like-a-son-of-man receiving eternal dominion from the Ancient-of-Days.

LUK 9:29.notes: HAPAX ἐξαστράπτω (flashing like lightning) for Jesus's garments echoes LXX Dan 10:6 (angel-of-the-Lord: ὡς ὅρασις ἀστραπῆς) and Ezek 1:4,14 (ὡς εἶδος λαμπάδων ... ὡς εἶδος τοῦ βεζέκ). Theophanic-radiance vocabulary.

LUK 9:31.notes: Use of ἔξοδος for Jesus's death/ascension-journey in Jerusalem deliberately echoes the LXX title of Exodus (ταῦτα τὰ ὀνόματα τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ... = Ἔξοδος). Frames Jesus's Passion as the great new-Exodus, with Moses (original Exodus-leader) present as witness.

LUK 9:33.notes: Peter's proposal to make three σκηναί (tents/booths) for Jesus, Moses, and Elijah evokes the Feast of Tabernacles/Sukkot (Lev 23:42-43) when Israel dwelt in booths commemorating wilderness-wandering.

LUK 9:34.notes: The overshadowing-cloud (νεφέλη ... ἐπεσκίαζεν) echoes the shekinah-cloud filling the tabernacle (Exo 40:34-38), pillar-of-cloud (Exo 13:21), and Sinai-cloud (Exo 24:15-18). Theophanic-cloud imagery.

LUK 9:35.notes: Three-fold composite divine-declaration: (1) ὁ υἱός μου = Ps 2:7 (royal-Messianic son); (2) ὁ ἐκλελεγμένος = Isa 42:1 LXX ὁ ἐκλεκτός μου (Isaianic Suffering-Servant — Luke's distinctive reading vs. Matt/Mark's ὁ ἀγαπητός); (3) αὐτοῦ ἀκούετε = Deut 18:15 LXX αὐτοῦ ἀκούσεσθε (prophet-like-Moses promise). TEXTUAL VARIANT note: SBLGNT retains ἐκλελεγμένος; Byz has ἀγαπητός (harmonization).

LUK 9:41.notes: γενεὰ ἄπιστος καὶ διεστραμμένη directly echoes Deut 32:5,20 LXX (γενεὰ σκολιὰ καὶ διεστραμμένη — Moses' Song about faithless-Israel in the wilderness). Also Deut 32:20 ὅτι γενεὰ ἐξεστραμμένη ἐστίν.

LUK 9:51.notes: Double-allusion: (1) τὸ πρόσωπον ἐστήρισεν echoes Ezek 21:2 LXX στήρισον τὸ πρόσωπόν σου (prophetic-determination-formula for facing judgment); (2) ἀνάλημψις (HAPAX) echoes Elijah's being-taken-up in 2 Kings 2:9-11 LXX (ἀναλαμβάνω). Frames Jesus's Passion-Resurrection-Ascension as his "taking-up," with prophetic-face-set resolve.

LUK 9:54.notes: James and John's request πῦρ καταβῆναι ἀπὸ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ ἀναλῶσαι αὐτούς directly alludes to Elijah calling fire from heaven on Ahaziah's messengers (2 Kings 1:10-12). TEXTUAL VARIANT: Byz/TR explicitly adds ὡς καὶ Ἠλίας ἐποίησεν ("as Elijah also did"); SBLGNT/NA28 omits as secondary-harmonization.

LUK 9:60.notes: Jesus's radical-prioritization of kingdom-proclamation over burying one's father echoes Deut 33:9 (Levi, who disregarded father/mother to keep God's covenant). The Levitical-model of undivided-service becomes the disciple's model.

LUK 9:61.notes: The would-be-disciple's request to first-bid-farewell-to-family directly echoes Elisha asking Elijah for the same permission in 1 Kings 19:19-21. Elijah granted Elisha's request; Jesus does NOT grant this request — a deliberate more-stringent-than-Elijah move.

LUK 9:62.notes: HAPAX ἄροτρον (plow). The plow-image directly echoes Elisha plowing with twelve-yoke-of-oxen when Elijah called him (1 Kings 19:19-21). Jesus's saying inverts the OT-precedent: where Elijah permitted farewell, Jesus insists on undivided-forward-focus.

LUK 10:1.notes: The sending of the seventy-two (or seventy per MS variant) echoes Num 11:16-25 (Moses appointing 70 elders to share the Spirit and burden of leadership). Num 11:26-27 adds Eldad and Medad who prophesied in the camp outside the tent, possibly bringing the count to 72. If Luke reads 72 (SBLGNT/NA28), the number also matches LXX Gen 10's 72 nations-of-the-world (vs. MT's 70), framing the mission as anticipation of universal/Gentile mission. Either number carries typological significance — Jesus as the new Moses commissioning Israel-representing-and-extending envoys.

LUK 10:15.notes: Direct verbal echo of Isa 14:13-15 LXX (Babylon-taunt-song): εἰς τὸν οὐρανὸν ἀναβήσομαι... εἰς ᾅδου καταβιβασθήσῃ. Luke's καταβιβασθήσῃ matches the LXX verb exactly. Jesus applies the Babylon-taunt to Capernaum (his Galilean ministry base, Luke 4:31). Intertextual claim: Capernaum has placed itself in the position of Babylon's prideful king before imminent divine-descent to Sheol. Parallels Matt 11:23.

LUK 10:18.notes: Lightning-fall imagery of Satan evokes Isa 14:12 LXX (πῶς ἐξέπεσεν ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ὁ Ἑωσφόρος ὁ πρωὶ ἀνατέλλων — 'How has fallen from heaven the morning-star'), read from 2nd-century CE onward as Satan's primordial fall. Also connects to Jewish apocalyptic tradition (1 Enoch 86, 88; Rev 12:7-9). ἐθεώρουν imperf = Jesus sees Satan's cosmic-defeat actualized proleptically in the 72's exorcisms.

LUK 10:19.notes: Authority to tread on snakes-and-scorpions echoes Ps 91:13 LXX (Psalm 90:13 LXX): ἐπ᾽ ἀσπίδα καὶ βασιλίσκον ἐπιβήσῃ, καὶ καταπατήσεις λέοντα καὶ δράκοντα. Jesus extends the psalmist's promise-of-protection to his missionaries. Note the Ps 91 inclusio: Satan quoted Ps 91:11-12 in Luke 4:9-11 to tempt Jesus; Jesus now rightly applies the following verse (91:13) to his delegates' mission. Possible secondary-echo of Gen 3:15 (seed crushing serpent's head).

LUK 10:20.notes: 'Names written in heaven' echoes the OT book-of-life tradition: Exod 32:32 (Moses: 'blot me out of your book'); Ps 69:28; Dan 12:1 ('everyone whose name is found written in the book'); Mal 3:16 ('a book of remembrance was written before him'). The motif is elaborated in Rev 3:5, 13:8, 20:12-15, 21:27 (the Lamb's book-of-life); Phil 4:3. Jesus redirects the 72's joy from exorcistic-power to eschatological-security.

LUK 10:27.notes: COMPOSITE CITATION. The lawyer combines Deut 6:5 (Shema) and Lev 19:18 — the dual-summary of Torah also taught by Jesus himself at Mark 12:29-31 and Matt 22:37-40. Luke's form has FOUR elements (καρδία + ψυχή + ἰσχύς + διάνοια) for Deut 6:5, whereas MT Hebrew has three (לֵבָב נֶפֶשׁ מְאֹד). LXX manuscripts vary: some render MT מְאֹד as ἰσχύς, some as δύναμις; some add διάνοια as a fourth; some substitute διάνοια for καρδία. Luke's four-element form matches certain LXX traditions. Lev 19:18 ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν rendered verbatim from LXX.

LUK 10:28.notes: FULFILLMENT/PRINCIPLE-ALLUSION. 'Do this and you will live' paraphrases Lev 18:5 LXX (καὶ ποιήσας αὐτὰ ἄνθρωπος ζήσεται ἐν αὐτοῖς). Paul cites the same verse at Rom 10:5 and Gal 3:12 as the principle of law-righteousness. Jesus agrees formally with the lawyer's Torah-answer; the Samaritan-parable then exposes whether the lawyer can actually DO it.

LUK 10:33.notes: The Samaritan-as-exemplary-neighbor enacts Lev 19:18 love-of-neighbor in a way that reverses first-century Jewish assumptions about who qualifies as 'neighbor.' Rabbinic debates variously limited πλησίον to fellow-Israelite; Jesus' parable redefines neighbor through action rather than ethnic or religious category-membership. The Samaritan (ethnically-religiously other) is the one who fulfills the commandment the priest and Levite (ethnically-religiously insider) fail to fulfill. See Luke 10:27 for the primary citation.

LUK 10:34.notes: The oil-and-wine-on-wounds care echoes Isa 1:6 LXX imagery of Israel's unbandaged wounds (ἀπὸ ποδῶν ἕως κεφαλῆς οὔτε τραῦμα οὔτε μώλωψ οὔτε πληγὴ φλεγμαίνουσα, οὐκ ἔστιν μάλαγμα ἐπιθεῖναι οὔτε ἔλαιον οὔτε καταδέσμους — 'from the feet to the head, there is no wound or bruise or inflamed blow; it is not possible to apply a plaster or oil or bandages'). The shared vocabulary (τραῦμα, ἔλαιον, καταδέω) is striking. Where Isa 1:6 describes Israel unbandaged, the Samaritan applies the very care Isaiah depicted as absent. Possible intertextual-gospel-message: the Samaritan models the compassion Israel's leaders failed to extend.

LUK 11:20.notes: 'Finger of God' (δάκτυλος θεοῦ) deliberately echoes Exod 8:19 LXX (Pharaoh's magicians: δάκτυλος θεοῦ ἐστιν τοῦτο, acknowledging the plagues) and Exod 31:18 / Deut 9:10 (the Ten Commandments written by God's finger). Luke's distinctive phrasing vs Matt 12:28's πνεύματι θεοῦ. The Exodus-allusion frames Jesus as the new-Moses bringing greater-exodus deliverance from demonic bondage. The kingdom-of-God 'has come upon you' (ἔφθασεν) = inaugurated-eschatology enacted through exorcism.

LUK 11:22.notes: The 'stronger-one overcoming the strong-man and dividing his spoils' echoes Isa 53:12 LXX (αὐτὸς ἁμαρτίας πολλῶν ἀνήνεγκεν... διὰ τοῦτο αὐτὸς κληρονομήσει πολλοὺς καὶ τῶν ἰσχυρῶν μεριεῖ σκῦλα — 'he will divide the spoils with the strong'). Divine-warrior imagery from Isa 49:24-25 ('will the captives of a warrior be taken? But thus says the LORD: even the captives of the warrior shall be taken') also resonates. Jesus applies divine-warrior-victor imagery to his own exorcism-ministry of liberating those held by Satan. Parallels Matt 12:29 / Mark 3:27.

LUK 11:29.notes: Introduces the 'sign of Jonah' motif developed in vv.30-32. OT referent = the book of Jonah as a whole. Jesus frames his generation's demand for a sign through the lens of Jonah's ministry — developed in v.30 as typological-equivalence. Parallels Matt 12:39, 16:4, Mark 8:12 (Mark truncates without interpretation).

LUK 11:30.notes: Luke's interpretation of the 'sign of Jonah' is Jonah's PREACHING to Nineveh (Jon 3) as typological-parallel to Jesus' preaching-to-this-generation. Distinct from Matt 12:40's three-days-in-fish typological-interpretation (which Luke does NOT include). Luke's reading suits his Gentile-mission theology: Jonah's word-to-pagans is paradigmatic-precedent for the Son-of-Man's word to his own Israel. Both Jonah-interpretations are authentic-synoptic-tradition preserved in parallel forms.

LUK 11:31.notes: Queen of Sheba's visit to Solomon (1 Kgs 10:1-13 / 2 Chr 9:1-12) — a paradigm-Gentile-seeker of divine-wisdom. Her eschatological-rising-to-condemn-this-generation damns-by-contrast: foreign-queen crossed-great-distance to hear Solomon's wisdom; Jesus' contemporaries reject the greater-wisdom speaking-to-them-directly. Parallels Matt 12:42. 'Ends of the earth' idiom echoes OT distance-formula (cf. Deut 28:49, Isa 5:26).

LUK 11:32.notes: Ninevites' repentance at Jonah's preaching (Jon 3) paradigm-Gentile-repenters. Eschatological-rising-to-condemn-this-generation by-contrast: pagan-Ninevites repented at the preaching-of-a-reluctant-prophet; Jesus' contemporaries reject the preaching of 'one-greater-than-Jonah.' Parallels Matt 12:41. Paired with v.31 as double-Gentile-testimony-against-Israel's-unresponsiveness.

LUK 11:40.notes: The ἄφρων ('fool') vocative echoes wisdom-literature condemnation of those-who-reject-God's-ways. Ps 14:1 LXX (13:1 LXX) εἶπεν ἄφρων ἐν καρδίᾳ αὐτοῦ· Οὐκ ἔστιν θεός ('the fool has said in his heart, there is no God') — same lexeme, same theological weight. Also Prov 1:7, 22-32 on fools rejecting wisdom. Jesus applies the wisdom-literature-condemnation of fools to the Pharisees — a sharp-rhetorical reversal since the Pharisees claimed wisdom-and-piety. The rhetorical-question 'did not the One who made the outside make the inside' echoes wisdom-creation-theology (Ps 139:13-16, Prov 20:12).

LUK 11:49.notes: 'I will send them prophets' is an OT prophetic-sending FORMULA attested across the Deuteronomistic-history and the prophetic corpus. 2 Chr 36:15-16 summarizes: 'the LORD... sent persistently to them by his messengers... but they kept mocking the messengers of God, despising his words, and scoffing at his prophets.' Cf. Jer 7:25 (ἀπέστειλα πρὸς ὑμᾶς πάντας τοὺς δούλους μου τοὺς προφήτας ἡμέρας καὶ ὄρθρου), Jer 25:4, 2 Kgs 17:13. Luke's 'wisdom of God said' presents this recurring-OT-formula as spoken-by-personified-divine-Wisdom. Matt 23:34 attributes the saying directly to Jesus without invoking 'wisdom.' Luke's addition 'and apostles' (ἀποστόλους) extends the Deuteronomistic-sending-formula forward to the New-Covenant apostolic-mission — a Luke-Acts distinctive framing.

LUK 11:50.notes: ἐκζητηθῇ τὸ αἷμα 'blood be sought-out/required' is technical-legal Hebraic language for divine-avenging of murder — Gen 9:5 LXX καὶ γὰρ τὸ ὑμέτερον αἷμα τῶν ψυχῶν ὑμῶν ἐκζητήσω ('for your blood of your lives I will require/seek-out'), the foundational post-Flood principle for human-bloodshed accountability. Extended to righteous-prophets' blood in 2 Kgs 9:7, Gen 42:22, Ps 9:12(11), Ezek 33:6. Jesus applies this foundational-principle to cumulative-blood-guilt of rejected-prophets falling on this-generation.

LUK 11:51.notes: FROM FIRST TO LAST MURDER IN THE JEWISH CANON. Abel = Gen 4:8-10 (first murder in Genesis, first book of Tanakh). Zechariah-ben-Jehoiada = 2 Chr 24:20-22 (killed in temple-court by King Joash's order — 'between altar and house' per 2 Chr 24:21). In the Jewish canonical-ordering, 2 Chronicles is the LAST book; so 'from Abel to Zechariah' = the entire biblical-history of murdered-innocents from first-to-last. This presupposes Jesus reading Scripture in the Tanakh-ordering. Matt 23:35 adds patronym 'son of Berechiah' (possibly conflating with prophet-Zechariah, Zech 1:1); Luke preserves the simpler attribution. ADDED in parallel with Matt 23:35.

LUK 12:5.notes: τὴν Γέενναν < Hebrew גֵּי־הִנֹּם Valley-of-Hinnom, where Ahaz/Manasseh sacrificed children to Molech (2 Chr 28:3; 33:6); Josiah desecrated it (2 Kgs 23:10); Jeremiah prophesied it would become valley-of-slaughter (Jer 7:31-32; 19:5-7). By second-temple era a burning refuse-dump, whence the metonym for eschatological fiery-judgment. Isa 66:24 LXX πῦρ ... οὐ σβεσθήσεται undergirds the unquenchable-fire-imagery. Luke preserves the Greek transliteration γέεννα; our Thai renders as นรก for reader-comprehension with the Hinnom-etymology documented in the verse-summary.

LUK 12:8.notes: ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ Ἀνθρώπου ὁμολογήσει ἐν αὐτῷ ἔμπροσθεν τῶν ἀγγέλων τοῦ Θεοῦ evokes Dan 7:13-14 LXX where the Son-of-Man enters the divine-court with myriads-of-attendants (Dan 7:10 θρόνοι ἐτέθησαν ... χίλιαι χιλιάδες ἐλειτούργουν αὐτῷ). Jesus switches from first-person to third-person Son-of-Man self-reference, maintaining the Danielic-title resonance while allowing ambiguity for outsiders. Court-room-witness frame (ἔμπροσθεν τῶν ἀγγέλων) mirrors Dan 7's heavenly-tribunal.

LUK 12:14.notes: Jesus' response τίς με κατέστησεν κριτὴν ἢ μεριστὴν ἐφ' ὑμᾶς echoes Exod 2:14 LXX τίς σε κατέστησεν ἄρχοντα καὶ δικαστὴν ἐφ' ἡμῶν, where an Israelite rejected Moses as judge-and-ruler. Ironic-inversion: Moses was REJECTED as judge; Jesus REFUSES to be one. Shared lexical core κατέστησεν ... κριτήν ... ἐφ' makes the Moses-echo deliberate.

LUK 12:15.notes: οὐκ ἐν τῷ περισσεύειν τινὶ ἡ ζωὴ αὐτοῦ ἐστιν ἐκ τῶν ὑπαρχόντων echoes Eccl 5:10 (5:9 LXX) ἀγαπῶν ἀργύριον οὐ πλησθήσεται ἀργυρίου — wealth does not supply life or fulfillment. Sirach 31:1-11 extends this wisdom tradition on wealth's inadequacy for life-sustenance. The aphorism's form is sapiential-proverbial.

LUK 12:16.notes: The rich-fool parable resonates with OT wisdom-echoes on foolish-rich-man dying-and-leaving-wealth-to-unknown-heir. Eccl 2:18-21 (Qoheleth hates his toil because he must leave it to one who comes after him who-may-be-a-fool); Eccl 5:13-15 (hoarded riches harm the owner; he takes nothing with him at death); Prov 11:28 (ὁ πεποιθὼς ἐπὶ πλούτῳ αὐτοῦ οὗτος πεσεῖται — the one trusting in his riches will fall); Ps 49:16-20 LXX (do not fear when a man becomes rich, for he will take nothing when he dies). Lukan-distinctive parable assimilates this entire wisdom-tradition.

LUK 12:19.notes: φάγε πίε εὐφραίνου imperative-string evokes Eccl 8:15 LXX (τοῦ φαγεῖν καὶ τοῦ πιεῖν καὶ τοῦ εὐφρανθῆναι, Qoheleth's observation about life-under-sun) AND Isa 22:13 LXX (φάγωμεν καὶ πίωμεν αὔριον γὰρ ἀποθνῄσκομεν — which Paul cites at 1 Cor 15:32 as paradigm-of-hedonism-if-no-resurrection). The rich-fool's self-address assimilates the Qoheleth-formula without its הֶבֶל-qualification, collapsing into pagan-Epicurean carpe-diem-without-wisdom. Luke's v.20 God-speech then realizes the Isaiah-tomorrow-we-die threat literally.

LUK 12:20.notes: ἄφρων-vocative + διελογίζετο ἐν ἑαυτῷ (v.17) echoes Ps 14:1/53:1 LXX εἶπεν ἄφρων ἐν καρδίᾳ αὐτοῦ — the biblical fool speaks-to-himself-ignoring-God. The rich-farmer IS the Psalmic fool, and God's direct-address via ἄφρων names the diagnosis. Wis 15:8 provides a Second-Temple parallel for ἀπαιτέω (demand-back) of soul-as-entrusted-capital returned to Creator.

LUK 12:24.notes: Lukan-distinctive κόρακες (where Matt 6:26 has generic τὰ πετεινὰ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ). Ravens were unclean by Lev 11:15 / Deut 14:14. OT feeding-tradition: 1 Kgs 17:4-6 (ravens feed Elijah at Cherith); Job 38:41 LXX τίς δὲ ἡτοίμασεν κόρακι βοράν; Ps 147:9 LXX διδόντι τοῖς κτήνεσι τροφὴν αὐτῶν καὶ τοῖς νεοσσοῖς τῶν κοράκων. Luke's κόρακας picks up the Psalmic/Wisdom tradition of God-feeding-unclean-ravens as evidence of universal divine care. A-fortiori: if God feeds unclean birds, how much more his covenant-children.

LUK 12:27.notes: ἐν πάσῃ τῇ δόξῃ αὐτοῦ evokes 1 Kgs 10:4-5 LXX (Queen-of-Sheba witnessing τὴν δόξαν Σολομῶν); 2 Chr 9:3-4 parallel. Solomon as proverbial exemplar of royal-splendor. Natural-creation's simpler-beauty surpassing Solomon's glory implicitly honors the Creator; cf. Luke 11:31 where 'one-greater-than-Solomon is here' is Jesus himself.

LUK 12:32.notes: τὸ μικρὸν ποίμνιον invokes the OT shepherd-flock motif: Ezek 34 (God as Shepherd over false-shepherds, gathering scattered flock); Isa 40:11 LXX ὡς ποιμὴν ποιμανεῖ τὸ ποίμνιον αὐτοῦ (the coming-Lord as tender shepherd); Ps 100:3 LXX πρόβατα νομῆς αὐτοῦ. 'Little-flock' carries the covenant-flock weight while acknowledging the numerically-small Jesus-movement of c. 30 CE. Father's εὐδόκησεν (gnomic-aorist) frames the kingdom-gift as enduring-divine-pleasure.

LUK 12:35.notes: αἱ ὀσφύες περιεζωσμέναι verbatim echoes Exod 12:11 LXX καὶ οὕτως φάγεσθε αὐτό, αἱ ὀσφύες ὑμῶν περιεζωσμέναι — Passover-readiness for imminent-Exodus-departure. Jesus transposes the Exodus-readiness-motif onto disciples-awaiting-Master's-return. Participle-periphrastic and lexical-agreement make this a direct verbal-echo, not a distant allusion. 1 Pet 1:13 ἀναζωσάμενοι τὰς ὀσφύας τῆς διανοίας draws from the same nexus.

LUK 12:40.notes: ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἔρχεται combines Dan 7:13 Son-of-Man-coming (ἰδοὺ μετὰ τῶν νεφελῶν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἐρχόμενος) with the thief-unexpectedness-metaphor. Parousia-imminence-without-calendar draws on Danielic expectation of decisive-Son-of-Man-in-glory, reframed for ongoing-disciple-vigilance.

LUK 12:49.notes: πῦρ motif linking Jesus' ministry to OT fire-of-judgment. Mal 4:1 (3:19 MT) ἰδοὺ ἡμέρα ἔρχεται καιομένη ὡς κλίβανος — imagery picked up by John the Baptist at Luke 3:16-17. Isa 66:15-16 eschatological fire-judgment on flesh. Luke 3:16 already pairs fire with Holy-Spirit-baptism (πνεύματι ἁγίῳ καὶ πυρί), integrating Pentecost-flame-imagery Acts 2:3 into the Luke-Acts narrative theology. Here πῦρ primarily indicates division-crisis (v.51 διαμερισμόν) and possibly eschatological judgment, with the paradox that the fire-bringer must first endure baptism-of-suffering (v.50).

LUK 12:53.notes: Explicit citation of Mic 7:6 LXX υἱὸς ἀτιμάζει πατέρα, θυγάτηρ ἐπαναστήσεται ἐπὶ τὴν μητέρα αὐτῆς, νύμφη ἐπὶ τὴν πενθεράν αὐτῆς. Luke EXPANDS Micah's asymmetric oppositions into SYMMETRIC bidirectional pairs (father-vs-son AND son-vs-father, mother-vs-daughter AND daughter-vs-mother, mother-in-law-vs-daughter-in-law AND daughter-in-law-vs-mother-in-law), heightening mutual alienation. Micah 7 diagnosed pre-exilic Israel's covenant breakdown; Jesus applies the same diagnostic to Messianic-era dividedness. Matt 10:35-36 preserves Micah's asymmetric pattern; Luke's symmetric form is distinctive.

LUK 12:56.notes: τὸν δὲ καιρὸν τοῦτον πῶς οὐκ οἴδατε δοκιμάζειν echoes the prophetic-critique of Israel for ignoring divinely-given signs. Isa 1:3 LXX ἔγνω βοῦς τὸν κτησάμενον καὶ ὄνος τὴν φάτνην τοῦ κυρίου αὐτοῦ, Ισραηλ δέ με οὐκ ἔγνω, καὶ ὁ λαός με οὐ συνῆκεν ('the ox knows its owner and the donkey its master's manger, but Israel does not know me, and my people do not understand'); Isa 6:9-10 hearing-without-understanding. Jesus applies the Isaianic diagnosis to the Pharisee-crowd who-can-read-weather-but-not-Messianic-signs.

LUK 13:1.notes: Pilate mixing Galileans' blood with their sacrifices evokes the OT motif of innocent blood defiling holy space: 2 Kings 16:3 (Ahaz's Molech child-sacrifices), Ps 79:3 LXX (they poured out their blood like water around Jerusalem, and there was no one to bury them), Jer 2:34 (on your skirts is found the lifeblood of the guiltless poor), Ezek 22:3-4 (city that sheds blood defiles itself). Luke frames the atrocity in prophetic-judgment register: blood-guilt is the covenantal-catastrophe that precipitates judgment on the whole people — setting up Jesus's repent-or-perish call.

LUK 13:6.notes: Fig-tree-in-vineyard parable draws on the established Israel-as-fig-tree imagery: Hos 9:10 (I found Israel like grapes in the wilderness, like first-ripe fruit on the fig tree); Mic 7:1 (no first-ripe fig my soul craved); Jer 8:13 (no grapes on the vine, no figs on the fig tree, their leaves withered); Joel 1:7 (he has laid waste my vine, splintered my fig tree). The vineyard-frame is borrowed from Isa 5:1-7 Song-of-the-Vineyard (Israel as God's vineyard bearing only wild grapes). Jesus compresses the cluster into a single parable of patience-before-judgment.

LUK 13:14.notes: The synagogue ruler's 'six days for work' (Ἓξ ἡμέραι εἰσὶν ἐν αἷς δεῖ ἐργάζεσθαι) echoes the Decalogue's fourth commandment: Exod 20:9 ἓξ ἡμέρας ἐργᾷ καὶ ποιήσεις πάντα τὰ ἔργα σου / Deut 5:13 ἓξ ἡμέρας ἐργᾷ. The citation is appealed to in order to criticize Jesus's Sabbath-healing; Jesus in response will reframe the Sabbath by appeal to Exodus-liberation-theology (Deut 5:15).

LUK 13:16.notes: Jesus's argument for Sabbath-healing-as-liberation rests on a cluster: Deut 5:15 (the Deuteronomic Sabbath grounded in Exodus-deliverance — the day of memory-of-slavery-ended); Isa 58:6 LXX λῦε πάντα σύνδεσμον ἀδικίας... ἀπόστελλε τεθραυσμένους ἐν ἀφέσει (the fast God chooses: to LOOSE the bonds of wickedness, to SET-FREE the oppressed — lexical-echo with ἔδησεν/λυθῆναι); Isa 61:1 LXX κηρύξαι αἰχμαλώτοις ἄφεσιν (proclaim release to captives — Luke 4:18 Nazareth-manifesto programmatic-text); Gen 17:7 (Abrahamic-covenant extends to descendants — 'daughter of Abraham' invokes covenant-inclusion). Jesus recasts Sabbath as the DAY OF RELEASE — fulfilling rather than violating the commandment.

LUK 13:19.notes: Mustard-seed-becomes-tree with birds-nesting-in-branches deliberately echoes the empire-as-cosmic-tree imagery: Dan 4:12, 21 LXX (Nebuchadnezzar's tree, birds of heaven dwelt in its branches, all flesh fed from it); Ezek 17:23 LXX (Messianic cedar under which every bird of every kind will dwell — direct background for the messianic-kingdom-as-tree reading); Ezek 31:6 (pharaoh-cedar with birds nesting in branches); Ps 104:12 LXX (birds of heaven dwell by waters and sing among the branches). The imperial-tree metaphor is co-opted for God's kingdom — subverting Daniel's Babylonian-tree reading.

LUK 13:21.notes: Three seahs of flour (ἀλεύρου σάτα τρία) matches the amount Sarah prepared for the three divine visitors at Mamre (Gen 18:6 LXX τρία μέτρα σεμιδάλεως). The hospitality-association suggests the hidden-leaven-transformation as new-Abrahamic-household-making: the kingdom silently transforms a community the way Sarah's baking fed the divine encounter. Hyperbolically large quantity (≈22 liters) matches the extravagance of Abraham's welcome.

LUK 13:25.notes: The householder's 'I do not know you' (οὐκ οἶδα ὑμᾶς) uses covenantal-knowing vocabulary: Amos 3:2 LXX ὑμᾶς ἔγνων ἐκ πασῶν φυλῶν τῆς γῆς (only you have I known from all families of the earth — covenantal-knowing = covenantal-recognition). Jer 24:5-10 (God 'knows' the good figs and rejects the bad). The formula is echoed in Matt 25:12 (ten-virgins parable) and Matt 7:23 (Lord-Lord pleaders). 'Do-not-know' is not memory-gap but covenantal-rejection.

LUK 13:27.notes: ἀπόστητε ἀπ’ ἐμοῦ, πάντες ἐργάται ἀδικίας directly cites LXX Ps 6:9 (ET 6:8) ἀπόστητε ἀπ’ ἐμοῦ, πάντες οἱ ἐργαζόμενοι τὴν ἀνομίαν. Luke's ἀδικίας replaces LXX ἀνομίαν (minor-lexical-variation, same semantic-field of covenantal-wickedness). Matthew's parallel (7:23) retains ἀνομίαν verbatim. Jesus-as-Judge assumes the Davidic-voice rejecting enemies in the psalm, a Christological move: the rejected-becomes-rejector, the psalmist-becomes-the-Lord-speaking-through-the-psalmist.

LUK 13:28.notes: Weeping and gnashing of teeth (κλαυθμὸς καὶ ὁ βρυγμὸς τῶν ὀδόντων) echoes Ps 112:10 LXX (ET 112:10) ὁ ἁμαρτωλὸς ὄψεται καὶ ὀργισθήσεται, τοὺς ὀδόντας αὐτοῦ βρύξει καὶ τακήσεται (the sinner will see and be angry, he will gnash his teeth and melt away) — same verb βρύχω + ὀδόντας. The feast-imagery of the Abrahamic-reunion in the kingdom draws from Isa 25:6-8 LXX (the Lord will make for all peoples on this mountain a feast of fat things). Matt 8:11-12 is near-parallel logion (at centurion-faith episode rather than Travel-Narrative).

LUK 13:29.notes: Four-directions gathering (ἀπὸ ἀνατολῶν καὶ δυσμῶν καὶ ἀπὸ βορρᾶ καὶ νότου) echoes Ps 107:3 LXX ἀπὸ ἀνατολῶν καὶ δυσμῶν, βορρᾶ καὶ θαλάσσης (Luke: νότου 'south' for LXX θαλάσσης 'sea' — a variant-pairing tradition). Isa 43:5-6 (I will bring your seed from the east + from the west + say to the north 'give them up' + to the south 'do not keep them back'); Isa 49:12 (they shall come from afar — north + west + from the land of Sinim); Mal 1:11 (from the rising of the sun to its setting my name will be great among the nations). Cluster of Gentile-ingathering texts.

LUK 13:32.notes: 'On the third day I am perfected' (τῇ τρίτῃ τελειοῦμαι) echoes Hos 6:2 LXX ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ ἀναστησόμεθα (on the third day we shall be raised up) — the passion-resurrection schema is embedded in the idiom of 'today-tomorrow-third-day.' Ezek 37:3-14 (valley of dry bones — third-day-pattern of national-resurrection). Jesus's ambiguous τελειοῦμαι lets the reader hear both 'I complete my ministry' and 'I am consummated/perfected (via passion-and-resurrection).'

LUK 13:33.notes: 'It is not admissible for a prophet to perish outside of Jerusalem' alludes to the Jerusalem-prophet-murder tradition: 2 Chr 24:20-22 (Zechariah ben-Jehoiada stoned in the temple court — the final OT martyr, cited by Jesus in Matt 23:35 / Luke 11:51 as the CANONICAL-BRACKET with Abel); Jer 26:20-23 (Uriah the prophet executed in Jerusalem under Jehoiakim). The pseudepigraphic Martyrdom-of-Isaiah continues this tradition (Isaiah sawn-asunder). Jesus's irony: Jerusalem is the EXPECTED place for prophet-murder — 'of course my death will happen there.'

LUK 13:34.notes: Hen-gathering-chicks-under-wings simile draws on the extensive OT wings-of-refuge cluster: Deut 32:11 LXX (eagle covers her nestlings, spreads her wings, takes them up); Ruth 2:12 (under whose wings you have come to take refuge); Ps 17:8 (hide me under the shadow of your wings); Ps 36:7 LXX (children of men take refuge under the shadow of your wings); Ps 57:1; Ps 61:4 (I will take refuge in the shelter of your wings); Ps 91:4 (he will cover you with his feathers, under his wings you will find refuge). Jesus assumes YHWH-as-mother-bird role, a strong Christological move. Prophet-killing echoes 2 Chr 24:20-22. Parallel to Matt 23:37 (near-verbatim logion).

LUK 13:35.notes: εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι κυρίου is a DIRECT citation of Ps 118:26 LXX (verbatim). Same verse shouted by the crowds at the Triumphal Entry (Luke 19:38), creating a prophetic-inclusio: Jesus predicts in 13:35 what will be fulfilled in 19:38. House-forsaken theme draws from Jer 12:7 LXX (ἐγκατέλιπον τὸν οἶκόν μου — I have forsaken my house); Jer 22:5 (this house will become a desolation); 1 Kings 9:7-8 (if you turn away from me, I will cut off Israel, this house will become a heap of ruins). TEXTUAL NOTE: SBLGNT/NA28 omit ἔρημος 'desolate' (Byz/TR adds — assimilation to Matt 23:38). We follow SBLGNT without ἔρημος.

LUK 14:14.notes: ἀνάστασις τῶν δικαίων ('resurrection of the righteous') alludes to Dan 12:2 LXX (καὶ πολλοὶ τῶν καθευδόντων ἐν γῆς χώματι ἐξεγερθήσονται, οὗτοι εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον... = 'many of those sleeping in the dust of the earth shall arise, these to eternal life...'). Dan 12:2 is the clearest OT affirmation of bodily resurrection with moral differentiation (righteous vs. unrighteous). Jesus's phrase 'resurrection of the righteous' (distinct from 'resurrection of the unrighteous') may imply a two-stage schema (cf. Luke 20:35 'those considered worthy to attain... the resurrection from the dead' — selective; Rev 20:4-6 first vs. second resurrection). The phrase assumes the Pharisaic/Second-Temple belief in bodily resurrection against Sadducees (cf. Luke 20:27-40; Acts 23:8). Jesus substitutes this eschatological reciprocity for earthly quid-pro-quo as the disciple's horizon of reward.

LUK 14:15.notes: The guest's beatitude 'Blessed is everyone who will eat at the feast in the kingdom of God' (μακάριος ὅστις φάγεται ἄρτον ἐν τῇ βασιλείᾳ τοῦ θεοῦ) invokes the Second-Temple-era eschatological-banquet hope rooted in Isa 25:6 LXX (καὶ ποιήσει κύριος σαβαωθ πᾶσι τοῖς ἔθνεσιν ἐπὶ τὸ ὄρος τοῦτο πίονται εὐφροσύνην, πίονται οἶνον, χρίσονται μύρον = 'The LORD of hosts will make for all peoples on this mountain [a feast of fat things, feast of wine...]'). Cross-refs: 1 Enoch 62:14, 2 Baruch 29:4-5, Luke 13:29, Matt 8:11, 22:1-14, Rev 19:9 all develop this Messianic-banquet motif. The guest's confidence will be parabolically subverted (vv.16-24) — popular-Israel presumption of inclusion gives way to Jesus's warning that many presumed-inside find themselves outside.

LUK 17:18.notes: ἀλλογενής (NT f=1) is a near-technical LXX-term for 'foreigner/Gentile-of-another-people' — ~100x in LXX (Gen 17:27, Exod 12:43, Lev 22:10, Num 1:51, Deut 17:15 etc.), typically for those excluded from sacred-precincts or sacral-privileges. The word is famously inscribed on the Jerusalem Temple's Soreg-wall warning-inscription (two extant copies) prohibiting Gentile-entry: ΜΗΘΕΝΑ ΑΛΛΟΓΕΝΗ ΕΙΣΠΟΡΕΥΕΣΘΑΙ. Jesus's choice of this word for the grateful Samaritan is pointedly-ironic: the man institutionally-excluded-from-Temple-worship is the one who actually gave-glory-to-God (the very purpose of Temple-worship). Prefigures Luke's Acts-narrative of gospel going to ἔθνη/Gentiles.

LUK 17:26.notes: Jesus invokes Noah-typology (vv.26-27): the days-of-Noah as a time of routine normalcy before sudden catastrophic judgment, paralleled to the days-of-the-Son-of-Man. Per Matt 24:37-39 synoptic-parallel this is an established apocalyptic Jesus-tradition. Theological point: eschatological-catastrophe comes suddenly upon the routine-absorbed generation, as it did in Noah's day.

LUK 17:27.notes: εἰσῆλθεν Νῶε εἰς τὴν κιβωτόν + ἦλθεν ὁ κατακλυσμὸς καὶ ἀπώλεσεν πάντας echoes the Gen 7:7-23 LXX flood-destruction narrative. κιβωτός ('ark') and κατακλυσμός ('flood') are the LXX technical-terms for these (Gen 7:1-23 LXX passim). Synoptic-parallel: Matt 24:38-39.

LUK 17:28.notes: Jesus opens Lot/Sodom-typology (vv.28-30) paralleling the Noah-typology of vv.26-27. The list of six routine activities (eating, drinking, buying, selling, planting, building) characterizes Sodom's prosperous-normalcy right up to the destruction. Genesis 18-19 provides the narrative base for Sodom/Gomorrah as the paradigm of sudden-divine-judgment on wicked-prosperity.

LUK 17:29.notes: ἔβρεξεν πῦρ καὶ θεῖον ἀπ’ οὐρανοῦ directly echoes LXX Gen 19:24: καὶ κύριος ἔβρεξεν ἐπὶ Σόδομα καὶ Γόμορρα θεῖον καὶ πῦρ παρὰ κυρίου ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ('and the Lord rained on Sodom and Gomorrah sulfur and fire from the Lord from heaven'). Luke's verb ἔβρεξεν + nouns θεῖον + πῦρ + prep ἐκ/ἀπ' οὐρανοῦ preserve the LXX diction. The fire-and-sulfur-from-heaven becomes NT paradigm for eschatological-judgment (cf. ISA 34:9-10 LXX, REV 14:10, 19:20, 20:10). Luke shows characteristic LXX-dependent vocabulary here.

LUK 17:32.notes: Brief one-verse imperative 'remember Lot's wife' presupposes audience-knowledge of Gen 19:26 (καὶ ἐπέβλεψεν ἡ γυνὴ αὐτοῦ εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω καὶ ἐγένετο στήλη ἁλός — 'his wife looked back and became a pillar of salt'). Note the DELIBERATE verbal-echo: v.31 warns μὴ ἐπιστρεψάτω εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω ('do not turn-back to the things behind') — Gen 19:26 LXX ἐπέβλεψεν εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω ('she looked back to the things behind'). Same phrase εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω, echoing across the two verses by design. Jesus structures the Lot-typology with careful LXX-phraseology.

LUK 17:37.notes: The proverb ὅπου τὸ σῶμα, ἐκεῖ καὶ οἱ ἀετοὶ ἐπισυναχθήσονται echoes the Job 39:27-30 LXX description of the eagle/vulture: where the slain are, there it is (ἐν δὲ χώρᾳ οὗ ἂν ὦσι τεθνηκότες παραχρῆμα εὑρίσκονται — LXX Job 39:30). The imagery is proverbial rather than direct-quotation. Synoptic-parallel: Matt 24:28 uses identical saying. The vulture-gathering becomes a Jewish-apocalyptic motif (cf. Ezek 39:4, 17-20 for eschatological feast-of-birds after divine-judgment).

LUK 18:7.notes: 'Cry out day and night' (βοώντων αὐτῷ ἡμέρας καὶ νυκτός) echoes the lament-prayer posture of Ps 22:2 (MT 22:3 כָּל־הַיּוֹם ... וְלַיְלָה — 'all the day ... and night') and especially Ps 88:1 LXX 87:2 (ἡμέρας ἐκέκραξα καὶ ἐν νυκτὶ ἐναντίον σου — 'day I cried out and by night before you'). The day-and-night-crying-to-God motif is a stock lament-psalm feature (cf. Ps 42:3; 77:2; Lam 2:18) — not a specific quotation but a deliberate invocation of the persecuted-righteous-petitioner tradition. The parable's ἐκλεκτοί (elect) are placed in the same posture as the lament-psalmist.

LUK 18:20.notes: DIRECT citation of the second-table Decalogue: adultery (Exod 20:14 / Deut 5:18), murder (Exod 20:13 / Deut 5:17), theft (Exod 20:15 / Deut 5:19), false-witness (Exod 20:16 / Deut 5:20), honor-parents (Exod 20:12 / Deut 5:16). Luke's order (adultery-murder-steal-witness-honor) matches LXX-B Deuteronomy order and Matt 19:18 (vs MT-Exod order murder-adultery-steal). Luke, like Mark 10:19 and Matt 19:18, omits the 10th commandment (do-not-covet) — which would have cut directly to the rich ruler's failure. Luke does NOT include Mark's added μὴ ἀποστερήσῃς (do-not-defraud) nor Matthew's added Lev 19:18 love-neighbor command. Luke's list is the shortest synoptic form — pure second-table-harm-prohibitions + parent-honor.

LUK 18:31.notes: 'Everything written by the prophets about the Son of Man will be fulfilled' (τελεσθήσεται πάντα τὰ γεγραμμένα διὰ τῶν προφητῶν) — Luke's third passion-prediction (cf. LUK 9:22, 9:44) makes explicit the prophetic-fulfillment framework. This is a META-CLAIM over a corpus of prophetic texts, not a single-verse citation: (1) Isa 52:13-53:12 (suffering-servant, delivered over, pierced, vindicated on the third day via Hos 6:2); (2) Ps 22 (mockery, spitting, piercing); (3) Zech 12:10 (pierced one); (4) Dan 7:13-14 (Son-of-Man coming with authority). Luke reinforces this claim post-resurrection at 24:25-27 and 24:44-47 ('everything written about me in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms'). Classified as fulfillment_allusion since it invokes a prophetic complex rather than quoting any single text.

LUK 19:9.notes: Allusion to the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 17). 'Son of Abraham' language draws on Second-Temple debate over who counts as covenant-member (cf. LUK 3:8 John Baptist; 13:16 daughter of Abraham; JHN 8:39). Jesus affirms Zacchaeus as genuine covenant-member against the Pharisaic exclusion of tax collectors — repentance-evidenced-by-fruit defines Abrahamic descent more than mere lineage.

LUK 19:10.notes: Allusion to Ezek 34:16 LXX τὸ ἀπολωλὸς ζητήσω ('I will seek the lost'). In Ezek 34 YHWH promises to personally shepherd Israel after the failed human shepherds. Jesus' mission-statement takes on the divine-shepherd role. The lexical pair ζητέω + ἀπολωλώς is the Ezekiel-signature. Not direct-citation (no ὅτι-formula or γέγραπται marker) but long-recognized allusion. Connects to Luke 15's three lost-parables.

LUK 19:38.notes: DIRECT CITATION of Ps 118:26 LXX (117:26 LXX): Εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι κυρίου. Luke INSERTS βασιλεύς ('king') between ἐρχόμενος and ἐν — a Christological editorial highlight making explicit what the disciples mean. The Hallel-psalm (113-118) was sung at Passover-pilgrimage; the verse functioned as the traditional greeting to pilgrims arriving at the temple. Jesus' disciples apply it as Messiah-King greeting. PARALLEL: MRK 11:9, MAT 21:9, JHN 12:13 all cite the same verse; Matthew adds 'Hosanna to the Son of David'; John follows with 'the King of Israel' as commentary. LUCAN INCLUSIO: LUK 2:14 (angels: δόξα ἐν ὑψίστοις θεῷ καὶ ἐπὶ γῆς εἰρήνη) ↔ LUK 19:38 (disciples: ἐν οὐρανῷ εἰρήνη καὶ δόξα ἐν ὑψίστοις) — same key words, positions swapped.

LUK 19:40.notes: Allusion to Hab 2:11 — 'the stone will cry out from the wall.' In Habakkuk the stones cry as witness against oppressors; Jesus redeploys the image for stones crying in praise of Messiah. Creates ironic-bookend with v.44 ('they will not leave stone upon stone in you'): stones praising (v.40) and stones falling (v.44) frame the rejection-of-Messiah scene.

LUK 19:41.notes: Prophetic-weeping echoes. Jer 9:1 LXX 'τίς δώσει τῇ κεφαλῇ μου ὕδωρ καὶ τοῖς ὀφθαλμοῖς μου πηγὴν δακρύων; καὶ κλαύσομαι' (who will give water to my head and fountain of tears to my eyes, and I will weep). Isa 22:4 'let me alone, I will weep bitterly.' Both set the paradigm of prophetic-lament over Jerusalem's coming destruction that Jesus enacts at Luke 19:41. Not direct-citation; conceptual-allusion grounded in the shared prophet-weeping-over-Jerusalem trope. Reinforces that Jesus' tears are prophetic-compassionate, not emotional-collapse.

LUK 19:43.notes: Military-siege language (παρεμβάλλω, χάραξ, περικυκλόω) echoes LXX siege-descriptions. Isa 29:3 LXX κυκλώσω σε χάρακα of Jerusalem siege; Ezek 4:2 παρεμβολαῖς / χάραξ of the symbolic-siege sign-act. Luke's triple-hapax concentration here deliberately invokes prophetic-siege-vocabulary. AD 70 fulfillment: Titus' Roman siege (Josephus Wars 5.12) included circumvallation with exactly these features.

LUK 19:44.notes: ἐδαφίζω ('dash to ground') echoes Ps 137:9 LXX (136:9 LXX) of dashing Babylonian babies on rocks — the captivity-lament. Also Hos 10:14, Nah 3:10 similar razing-to-ground. τέκνα σου ἐν σοί applies the metaphor to Jerusalem's inhabitants (inversion: Jerusalem, formerly victim, is now the target of the same fate). Secondary connection: the ἐπισκοπή-theme recapitulates Luke's visitation-inclusio (1:68, 1:78, 7:16, now 19:44).

LUK 19:46.notes: Composite citation. (1) ὁ οἶκός μου οἶκος προσευχῆς = LXX Isa 56:7 verbatim (minus 'παντὶ τῷ λαῷ' / 'for all nations' that Mark retains; Luke omits — focus is commerce-vs-prayer, not Gentile-inclusion here). (2) σπήλαιον λῃστῶν = LXX Jer 7:11 verbatim. JEREMIAH 7 CONTEXT: prophetic-temple-denunciation of those who treat temple as safe-haven despite systematic injustice outside. Jesus applies Jeremiah's whole-temple-indictment to the commercial-misuse. ISAIAH 56 CONTEXT: eschatological-inclusion of foreigners/eunuchs at God's house. Luke's selective-quotation (omitting 'for all nations') keeps focus on the house-of-prayer function against commerce. PARALLEL: MRK 11:17 (includes 'for all nations'), MAT 21:13 (omits 'for all nations', like Luke). LUK-MAT agreement here is notable.

LUK 20:9.notes: The parable of the wicked tenants (Luke 20:9-19 || Mark 12:1-12 || Matt 21:33-46) draws its ἀμπελών-framework from Isaiah 5:1-7 (LXX vocabulary-cluster: ἀμπελῶνα ἐγενήθη τῷ ἠγαπημένῳ / φυτεύω / φραγμός / ληνός / πύργος). Isaiah casts Israel as God's vineyard which fails to yield justice (Isa 5:7 κρίσιν, 7:5 δικαιοσύνην); Jesus redirects the indictment from the vineyard-itself (Isaiah) to the tenant-leadership (parable). Audience would recognize the Isaianic-framework immediately. Luke's compact retelling (omitting Mark's φραγμός/ληνός/πύργος details) focuses exclusively on the tenant-conflict rather than the vineyard-structure, sharpening the leadership-indictment.

LUK 20:17.notes: Psalm 118:22 (LXX 117:22) verbatim quotation: λίθον ὃν ἀπεδοκίμασαν οἱ οἰκοδομοῦντες οὗτος ἐγενήθη εἰς κεφαλὴν γωνίας. Psalm 118 is the final Hallel-psalm (113-118), sung at Passover and Tabernacles — every hearer would recognize the line immediately. Messianic-interpretation of this verse predates Jesus: Targum Ps 118:22 identifies the rejected-stone with David/Messiah, and the stone-imagery is picked up throughout NT (Acts 4:11; Rom 9:33; 1 Pet 2:4-8 combines with Isa 28:16 + Isa 8:14 in a stone-testimonia-collection). Luke renders identical to MRK 12:10 synoptic-parallel and MAT 21:42. After the parable's indictment, the citation applies the stone-metaphor: the rejected-heir (parable) becomes the cornerstone (Psalm 118) — death becomes vindication.

LUK 20:18.notes: Jesus extends the Ps 118:22 stone-metaphor in two directions: (1) ISA 8:14-15 — Yahweh is a 'stone of stumbling and rock of offense' that makes many fall, stumble, and be broken (συνθλάω-cognate vocabulary LXX Isa 8:15 συντριβήσονται); (2) DAN 2:34-35, 44-45 — the stone cut-without-hands that falls on and pulverizes (LXX λικμᾶν Dan 2:35 of chaff-dispersal) the statue representing earthly-kingdoms, establishing God's eternal-kingdom. Jesus' λικμήσει exactly echoes LXX Dan 2:35. The composite shows dual-effect of rejecting the Messianic-stone: falling-over-it (Isaiah passive-encounter) = shattered; being-fallen-on-by-it (Daniel active-judgment) = pulverized. Both outcomes = judgment. The double-OT-allusion functions as Jesus' warning-commentary on his Ps 118 citation in v.17.

LUK 20:28.notes: Sadducee-citation of Levirate-marriage law (yibbum) from Deuteronomy 25:5-6 LXX: ἐὰν δὲ κατοικῶσιν ἀδελφοὶ ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτὸ καὶ ἀποθάνῃ εἷς ἐξ αὐτῶν καὶ σπέρμα μὴ ᾖ αὐτῷ...ὁ ἀδελφὸς τοῦ ἀνδρὸς αὐτῆς εἰσελεύσεται πρὸς αὐτὴν καὶ λήμψεται αὐτὴν ἑαυτῷ γυναῖκα... Luke's wording is paraphrase, not verbatim LXX, but clearly invokes the Deut law. Sadducee-strategy: cite Pentateuch (their accepted canon) to construct a case whose resolution-requires resurrection, then claim the resurrection-leads-to-absurdity. Jesus responds (vv.34-38) not by reinterpreting Levirate-law but by showing resurrection-life is ontologically-different from earthly-marriage-life — the presupposition of the trap was wrong. Also connected to GEN 38:8 (Onan refusing Levirate) + RUT 4 (Boaz as kinsman-redeemer via Levirate-analogous-custom).

LUK 20:37.notes: Exodus 3:6 burning-bush theophany (LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ θεὸς τοῦ πατρός σου, θεὸς Ἀβραὰμ καὶ θεὸς Ἰσαὰκ καὶ θεὸς Ἰακώβ). Jesus' quotation preserves the three patriarch-names and the θεός-thrice-repetition, while adapting the framing: he uses κύριον (Lord = YHWH euphemism) and restructures the first-person-divine-statement into a third-person-description-of-Moses'-language. Argumentum from verb-tense: God in Exodus-present says 'I AM' (not 'I WAS') the God of Abraham — requiring Abraham to be in present-existent-relation, hence resurrection. Rabbinic-precedent for this exegetical-move: b. Sanhedrin 90b (Rabbi Simai's proof from Exod 6:4 'to give them the land'); m. Sanhedrin 10:1 notes resurrection-from-Torah as required-belief. Jesus uses the Sadducees' accepted-canon (Pentateuch only) against them. Matches MRK 12:26 + MAT 22:32 synoptic-parallel citations.
