LUK 20:42.notes: Psalm 110:1 (LXX 109:1) verbatim quotation: εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου. The most-frequently-cited OT passage in NT (~25×): Matt 22:44; 26:64; Mark 12:36; 14:62; 16:19; Luke 20:42-43; 22:69; Acts 2:34-35; 5:31; 7:55-56; Rom 8:34; 1 Cor 15:25; Eph 1:20; Col 3:1; Heb 1:3, 13; 8:1; 10:12-13; 12:2; 1 Pet 3:22; Rev 3:21. Christology-foundation text. Jesus' argument: David (the psalm's speaker) calls the Messiah 'my Lord' — but in Jewish culture ancestors are greater than descendants, so how can David call his own descendant 'Lord'? Implicit answer: the Messiah is more than Davidic-descendant; he is pre-existent-divine-Lord. Rabbinic tradition already read Ps 110 messianically (b. Sanhedrin 38b; b. Nedarim 32b).

LUK 20:43.notes: Continuation of Ps 110:1 (LXX 109:1b): ἕως ἂν θῶ τοὺς ἐχθρούς σου ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου. Footstool-image signifies total subjection of enemies (ANE royal-iconography, Egyptian pharaohs depicted with feet on enemy-necks). The 'until' (ἕως ἂν) marks the messianic enthronement-reign culminating in eschatological-victory. Footstool-motif recurs in NT: 1 Cor 15:25 (πάντας τοὺς ἐχθροὺς ὑπὸ τοὺς πόδας), Heb 1:13 + 10:13 (ἕως τεθῶσιν οἱ ἐχθροὶ ὑποπόδιον). Matches MRK 12:36 + MAT 22:44 synoptic-parallel citations. Verse-division is editorial: in NA28/SBLGNT verse 43 continues the citation begun in v.42.

LUK 21:10.notes: ἐγερθήσεται ἔθνος ἐπ’ ἔθνος echoes LXX Isa 19:2 (ἐπεγερθήσεται Αἰγύπτιος ἐπ’ Αἰγύπτιον) + 2 Chron 15:6 (καὶ πολεμήσει ἔθνος πρὸς ἔθνος καὶ πόλις πρὸς πόλιν). Apocalyptic-chaos-formula for end-time political-convulsion. Same allusion operative at MRK 13:8 / MAT 24:7 synoptic-parallels.

LUK 21:11.notes: φόβητρον (hapax in NT) echoes LXX Isa 19:17 (καὶ ἔσται ἡ χώρα τῶν Ἰουδαίων τοῖς Αἰγυπτίοις εἰς φόβητρον) and Hos 6:10 (φρικτὰ εἴδον ... φόβητρα). Luke's Greek-OT-vocabulary selection signals Septuagintal-prophetic-coloring for the apocalyptic-sign-list. Pair λιμοί + λοιμοί (famines + pestilences) has Hellenistic historiographic parallels but no direct LXX-source — likely Luke's own rhetorical shaping.

LUK 21:15.notes: ἐγὼ γὰρ δώσω ὑμῖν στόμα καὶ σοφίαν echoes LXX Exod 4:12 (ἐγὼ ἀνοίξω τὸ στόμα σου καὶ συμβιβάσω σε ἃ μέλλεις λαλῆσαι) and Exod 4:15 (ἐγὼ ἀνοίξω τὸ στόμα σου καὶ τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ καὶ συμβιβάσω ὑμᾶς ἃ ποιήσετε). Christological-claim: Jesus promises in his own person what YHWH promised Moses. High-christology compressed into a trial-preparation exhortation.

LUK 21:16.notes: παραδοθήσεσθε...ὑπὸ γονέων καὶ ἀδελφῶν echoes LXX Mic 7:6 (υἱὸς ἀτιμάζει πατέρα, θυγάτηρ ἐπαναστήσεται ἐπὶ τὴν μητέρα αὐτῆς, νύμφη ἐπὶ τὴν πενθερὰν αὐτῆς, ἐχθροὶ ἀνδρὸς πάντες οἱ ἄνδρες οἱ ἐν τῷ οἴκῳ αὐτοῦ). Classic apocalyptic-motif of end-time family-dissolution. Luke expands Micah's three-pair family-conflict to a four-category list (parents, siblings, relatives, friends), broadening the betrayal-scope.

LUK 21:18.notes: θρὶξ ἐκ τῆς κεφαλῆς ὑμῶν οὐ μὴ ἀπόληται echoes the OT-divine-protection-formula 'not-one-hair-of-his-head-shall-fall-to-the-ground' (LXX 1 Sam 14:45 οὐδέ τι τῶν τριχῶν τῆς κεφαλῆς αὐτοῦ πεσεῖται; 2 Sam 14:11 εἰ πεσεῖται ἀπὸ τῆς τριχὸς τοῦ υἱοῦ σου ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν; 1 Kgs 1:52 εἰ πεσεῖται τῶν τριχῶν αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν). Jesus applies the OT-protection-formula in an eschatological-context where bodily-death is already predicted (v.16) — forcing spiritual/eschatological rather than physical-preservation reading. LUK 12:7 parallel.

LUK 21:20.notes: ἤγγικεν ἡ ἐρήμωσις αὐτῆς echoes the Danielic 'βδέλυγμα τῶν ἐρημώσεων / abomination-of-desolation' vocabulary-cluster (LXX Dan 9:27; 11:31; 12:11). Luke REPLACES Mark 13:14 / Matt 24:15 direct-citation of 'τὸ βδέλυγμα τῆς ἐρημώσεως' with the plain-historical description 'armies-surrounding-Jerusalem' — translating the Danielic prophecy for Greco-Roman readers unfamiliar with the Hebrew-Bible allusion. ἐρήμωσις-vocabulary retained as subtle-echo.

LUK 21:22.notes: ἡμέραι ἐκδικήσεως...τοῦ πλησθῆναι πάντα τὰ γεγραμμένα is the Lukan fulfillment-formula applied to OT 'days-of-vengeance' prophetic-tradition. LXX parallels: Deut 32:35 (ἐν ἡμέρᾳ ἐκδικήσεως); Isa 34:8 (ἡμέρα ἐκδικήσεως — Edom-oracle); Isa 61:2 (ἡμέραν...ἀνταποδόσεως); Isa 63:4 (ἡμέρα ἀνταποδόσεως — the Messiah's-treading-of-winepress); Jer 46:10 LXX 26:10 (ἡμέρα ἐκδικήσεως τῷ κυρίῳ — Egypt-oracle); Hos 9:7 (ἥκασιν αἱ ἡμέραι τῆς ἐκδικήσεως). Luke-Acts programmatic-theology of events-as-scripture-fulfillment (cf. LUK 18:31, 22:37, 24:25-27, 24:44-46).

LUK 21:23.notes: ἔσται...ἀνάγκη μεγάλη ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς καὶ ὀργὴ τῷ λαῷ τούτῳ echoes the prophetic Day-of-the-Lord ὀργή-formula: Zeph 1:15 LXX (ἡμέρα ὀργῆς ἡ ἡμέρα ἐκείνη ἡμέρα θλίψεως καὶ ἀνάγκης); Ezek 7:19 (ἐν ἡμέρᾳ ὀργῆς κυρίου); Isa 10:5-6 (the Assyrian-rod-of-God's-wrath on disobedient-people). Luke's specific phrasing λαῷ τούτῳ (this people) localizes the covenantal-wrath to the Jewish-people in context of AD-70 Jerusalem-siege. Historical AD 70: Josephus War 5-6 corroborates famine-induced suffering concentrated on most-vulnerable (pregnant + nursing).

LUK 21:24.notes: Ἰερουσαλὴμ ἔσται πατουμένη ὑπὸ ἐθνῶν ἄχρι οὗ πληρωθῶσιν καιροὶ ἐθνῶν — the only occurrence of phrase καιροὶ ἐθνῶν 'times-of-the-Gentiles' in NT. OT-background cluster: Dan 8:13 LXX (ἕως πότε ... ἡ θυσία ... δοθήσεται εἰς καταπάτημα); Dan 12:7 (ἕως καιροῦ καὶ καιρῶν καὶ ἥμισυ καιροῦ); Ps 79:1 LXX 78:1 (ἐμίαναν τὸν ναὸν τὸν ἅγιόν σου ... ἐποίησαν Ιερουσαλημ εἰς ὀπωροφυλάκιον); Isa 63:18 (κατεπάτησαν τὸ ἁγίασμά σου); Zech 12:3 (τίθησιν Ἰερουσαλὴμ λίθον καταπατούμενον). Paul's πλήρωμα τῶν ἐθνῶν (Rom 11:25) is the missiological-theological parallel — possibly informed by Luke's formulation (or shared tradition).

LUK 21:25.notes: σημεῖα ἐν ἡλίῳ καὶ σελήνῃ καὶ ἄστροις...συνοχὴ ἐθνῶν...ἤχους θαλάσσης καὶ σάλου — composite Day-of-the-Lord vocabulary. OT sun/moon/stars sign-cluster: Isa 13:10 (Babylon-oracle: οἱ γὰρ ἀστέρες τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ ὁ Ὠρίων...οὐ δώσουσιν τὸ φῶς αὐτῶν); Isa 34:4 (Edom-oracle: τακήσονται πᾶσαι αἱ δυνάμεις τῶν οὐρανῶν); Ezek 32:7-8 (Egypt-oracle: ὁ ἥλιος ἐν νεφέλῃ συγκαλύψω); Joel 2:10,30-31 (Day-of-the-Lord: ὁ ἥλιος καὶ ἡ σελήνη συσκοτάσουσιν); Joel 3:15 (same). OT sea-roaring tumult-cluster: Ps 46:2-3 MT = 45:3 LXX (ἠχήθησαν καὶ ἐταράχθησαν τὰ ὕδατα αὐτῶν); Isa 17:12-13 (ἠχὴ θαλασσῶν πολλῶν).

LUK 21:26.notes: αἱ δυνάμεις τῶν οὐρανῶν σαλευθήσονται echoes LXX Hag 2:6 (ἔτι ἅπαξ ἐγὼ σείσω τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν — Zion-oracle of all-nations-shaking; quoted directly Heb 12:26); Isa 34:4 (οἱ οὐρανοὶ ἑλιχθήσονται); Joel 2:10 (σεισθήσεται ὁ οὐρανός). Luke's Greek phrasing follows Mark 13:25 / Matt 24:29 verbatim — synoptic-locked Day-of-the-Lord formula.

LUK 21:27.notes: DIRECT OT-CITATION: τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐρχόμενον ἐν νεφέλῃ μετὰ δυνάμεως καὶ δόξης πολλῆς quotes Dan 7:13-14. LXX Dan 7:13: ἰδοὺ ἐπὶ τῶν νεφελῶν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἤρχετο; Theod: μετὰ τῶν νεφελῶν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἐρχόμενος ... καὶ αὐτῷ ἐδόθη ἡ ἀρχὴ καὶ ἡ τιμὴ καὶ ἡ βασιλεία. Luke's singular ἐν νεφέλῃ (vs. Mark/Matt's plural) is notable — possibly echoes LUK 9:34-35 Transfiguration-cloud-singular or Acts 1:9 Ascension-cloud-singular. Daniel's vision is ASCENSION-to-Father; Jesus reverses to RETURN-to-earth — christologically foundational transformation. 2nd-Temple messianic interpretation: 1 Enoch 46-48; 4 Ezra 13.

LUK 21:33.notes: ὁ οὐρανὸς καὶ ἡ γῆ παρελεύσονται, οἱ δὲ λόγοι μου οὐ μὴ παρελεύσονται — christological-claim echoing LXX Isa 40:6-8 (πᾶσα σὰρξ χόρτος ... ἐξηράνθη ὁ χόρτος καὶ τὸ ἄνθος ἐξέπεσε· τὸ δὲ ῥῆμα τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα); Isa 51:6 (ὁ οὐρανὸς ὡς καπνὸς ἐστερεώθη ... ἡ δὲ σωτηρία μου εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα ἔσται); Ps 102:25-27 LXX 101:26-28 (τὰ ἔργα τῶν χειρῶν σού εἰσιν οἱ οὐρανοί ... αὐτοὶ ἀπολοῦνται, σὺ δὲ διαμενεῖς). Jesus substitutes 'my words' for 'word of God / God himself' — implicit-divine-identity-claim. Synoptic-parallel MRK 13:31, MAT 24:35.

LUK 21:35.notes: ὡς παγίς ... ἐπὶ πάντας τοὺς καθημένους ἐπὶ πρόσωπον πάσης τῆς γῆς echoes LXX Isa 24:17-18 (φόβος καὶ βόθυνος καὶ παγὶς ἐφ' ὑμᾶς τοὺς κατοικοῦντας ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς). Isaiah 24 is the 'Little Apocalypse' (chs. 24-27) predicting universal-judgment on the whole inhabited earth. Hebrew alliterative-triplet pahad + pahat + pah (terror + pit + snare). Luke activates the Isaianic-apocalypse-frame: this eschaton-day is universal, not geographically-escapable, unlike vv.20-24's Judean-specific flight-to-mountains.

LUK 21:36.notes: σταθῆναι ἔμπροσθεν τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου echoes LXX Ps 130:3 (Ps 129:3: ἐὰν ἀνομίας παρατηρήσῃς, κύριε κύριε, τίς ὑποστήσεται;). 'Stand' as judicial-idiom for 'be-declared-innocent-when-judged' (cf. Ps 1:5; Mal 3:2; Rev 6:17). Combined with Dan 7:13-14 echo at v.27 (Son of Man coming), the closing exhortation frames disciples' prayer-and-watchfulness as preparation for vindication-before the eschatological-Judge.

LUK 22:20.notes: καινὴ διαθήκη ἐν τῷ αἵματί μου τὸ ὑπὲρ ὑμῶν ἐκχυννόμενον interweaves Mosaic covenant-blood (LXX Exod 24:8 ἰδοὺ τὸ αἷμα τῆς διαθήκης) + Jeremianic new-covenant (LXX Jer 31:31 [38:31] ἰδοὺ ἡμέραι ἔρχονται ... καὶ διαθήσομαι ... διαθήκην καινήν) + Zech 9:11 'blood-of-your-covenant' + Isa 53:12 'poured-out-his-soul-to-death.' Four-source confluence in a single eucharistic formula. Synoptic-parallel MRK 14:24 + MAT 26:28 similar but with τὸ αἷμα τῆς διαθήκης (sans 'new'). Luke + Paul distinctive ἡ καινὴ διαθήκη (cf. 1 Cor 11:25).

LUK 22:21.notes: ἡ χεὶρ τοῦ παραδιδόντος με μετ’ ἐμοῦ ἐπὶ τῆς τραπέζης echoes LXX Ps 40:10 (Ps 41:9 MT: ὁ ἐσθίων ἄρτους μου ἐμεγάλυνεν ἐπ’ ἐμὲ πτερνισμόν 'the-one-eating-my-bread has-lifted-his-heel-against-me'). Same Ps-41 explicitly-cited at JHN 13:18 in parallel context. Betrayer-who-shares-bread motif. Lukan rendering preserves the table-fellowship-image without explicit-citation-marker.

LUK 22:30.notes: καθήσεσθε ἐπὶ θρόνων τὰς δώδεκα φυλὰς κρίνοντες τοῦ Ἰσραήλ echoes Dan 7:9-14 Son-of-Man receives kingdom + Dan 7:22 'judgment-was-given-to-the-saints-of-the-Most-High-and-the-time-came-and-the-saints-possessed-the-kingdom' + Dan 7:27 'the-kingdom-and-the-dominion-will-be-given-to-the-people-of-the-saints-of-the-Most-High.' Possible additional-allusion to Wis 3:8 (deuterocanonical, non-authoritative per RULES.md §0): κρινοῦσιν ἔθνη καὶ κρατήσουσιν λαῶν. Synoptic-parallel MAT 19:28 same promise but Matthew places it during Galilean ministry.

LUK 22:31.notes: ὁ Σατανᾶς ἐξῃτήσατο ὑμᾶς echoes LXX Job 1:9-12 + 2:4-6 where Satan requests permission from God to test Job (Job 1:11 ἀποστεῖλον τὴν χεῖρά σου καὶ ἅψαι πάντων ὧν ἔχει; 1:12 'all that he has is in your hand'). The mid-voice ἐξαιτέομαι preserves the asking-for-custody-from-another's-authority sense characteristic of the Job-prologue. Theological-point: Satan's access-to-tempt is LIMITED — requires divine-permission (Job-theodicy-frame). The Satan-Job-sifting pattern illuminates Jesus' warning-to-Peter: a bounded-test is coming, and Peter's faith is under divine-protective-prayer (v.32).

LUK 22:37.notes: DIRECT OT-CITATION: καὶ μετὰ ἀνόμων ἐλογίσθη directly quotes LXX Isa 53:12 (καὶ ἐν τοῖς ἀνόμοις ἐλογίσθη). First-explicit-Lukan-application of the Suffering-Servant-of-Isaiah to Jesus; Luke-Acts will return to this (Acts 8:32-35 Philip + Ethiopian-eunuch reading Isa 53). Fulfilled literally at LUK 23:32-33 — Jesus crucified-between-two-λῃσταί (MRK 15:27 parallel; John 19:18). Luke's Suffering-Servant-Christology is foundational to his soteriology (cf. LUK 2:32 Nunc-Dimittis 'light-to-reveal-to-the-Gentiles' echoing Isa 42:6, 49:6).

LUK 22:42.notes: παρένεγκε τοῦτο τὸ ποτήριον ἀπ’ ἐμοῦ — 'cup' as OT symbol-of-divine-wrath-and-judgment. LXX Isa 51:17 (τὸ ποτήριον τοῦ θυμοῦ 'cup-of-wrath'), 51:22 (τὸ ποτήριον τῆς πτώσεως 'cup-of-staggering'); Jer 25:15-16 (LXX 32:15-16: τὸ ποτήριον τοῦ οἴνου τοῦ ἀκράτου τούτου 'cup-of-the-unmixed-wine' = Yahweh's-wrath against nations); Ezek 23:32-33 (cup-of-astonishment); Ps 75:8 LXX 74:9 (ποτήριον ... οἴνου ἀκράτου). Jesus prays-that-the-cup-of-divine-wrath (which-sinners-deserve) would-pass-from-him — acknowledging he is about to drink it on their behalf. Foundational to substitutionary-atonement-theology. Synoptic-parallel MRK 14:36, MAT 26:39.

LUK 22:43.notes: ὤφθη δὲ αὐτῷ ἄγγελος ἀπ’ οὐρανοῦ ἐνισχύων αὐτόν (textually-disputed, see RULES.md Tier-3 treatment) echoes the angelic-strengthening-of-servants-of-God motif: LXX Dan 10:18-19 (καὶ προσέθηκέ μοι καὶ ἥψατό μου ὡς ὅρασις ἀνθρώπου καὶ ἐνίσχυσέ με 'one-like-a-man touched me and strengthened me') and 1 Kgs 19:5-8 (angel-strengthens-Elijah under the broom-tree in his despair). Contrastive-echo to LUK 4:10-11 (Satan's taunt quoting Ps 91:11 'he-will-command-his-angels to-guard-you') — in Gethsemane, the angelic-guard materializes when the Son-of-God actually needs it, not at Satan's cynical prompt.

LUK 22:52.notes: Jesus' ὡς ἐπὶ λῃστήν-question points-forward to his crucifixion-between-two-λῃσταί (MRK 15:27, MAT 27:38; Luke has κακούργους 'criminals' at LUK 23:32-33). All three Synoptics invoke the Isa 53:12 'numbered-with-transgressors' fulfillment-frame. Luke uniquely makes the Isa 53:12 citation EXPLICIT at LUK 22:37 (see entry there); v.52 is the narrative-ratification of the citation — Jesus explicitly named the prophetic-lens under which his arrest unfolds. Cross-reference entry.

LUK 22:64.notes: The blindfolding + striking + mocking-prophetic-claim echoes LXX Isa 50:6 (τὸν νῶτόν μου δέδωκα εἰς μάστιγας τὰς δὲ σιαγόνας μου εἰς ῥαπίσματα τὸ δὲ πρόσωπόν μου οὐκ ἀπέστρεψα ἀπὸ αἰσχύνης ἐμπτυσμάτων 'I-gave-my-back-to-whippings and-my-cheeks-to-slaps; my-face-I-did-not-turn-from-the-shame-of-spittings'). Third-servant-song (Isa 50:4-9) features the Suffering-Servant enduring physical-abuse without retaliation. Luke's mockery-scene realizes the servant-pattern. Synoptic-parallel MRK 14:65, MAT 26:67-68.

LUK 22:69.notes: DIRECT OT-CITATION (fusion): ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου καθήμενος ἐκ δεξιῶν τῆς δυνάμεως τοῦ θεοῦ combines LXX Ps 110:1 [109:1] (εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου 'Sit at my right hand') + Dan 7:13-14 Son-of-Man receives-authority. Luke uses the same fusion earlier at LUK 20:41-44 (Jesus' Davidic-lordship puzzle) and LUK 21:27 (Son-of-Man coming-in-cloud from Dan 7). Enthronement-Christology. 'Power-of-God' (δύναμις τοῦ θεοῦ) = reverent circumlocution for 'God' (cf. rabbinic שְׁכִינָה / הַגְּבוּרָה). Synoptic-parallel MRK 14:62, MAT 26:64 — same citation-fusion but with additional 'coming-on-clouds-of-heaven' phrase that Luke omits.

LUK 22:70.notes: ἐγώ εἰμι in answer to 'are-you-the-Son-of-God?' potentially-echoes the divine-self-identification LXX Exod 3:14 (ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν 'I-am-the-one-who-is') and Isaiah's ἐγώ εἰμι self-declarations (Isa 43:10 ἐγώ εἰμι 'I-am' with continuation 'before-me-there-was-no-other-god and-after-me-there-will-be-none'; 43:13; 43:25). In context of the throne-claim at v.69 (echoing Ps 110:1 + Dan 7:13-14), the ἐγώ εἰμι response carries divine-overtones sufficient to ground the blasphemy-verdict in v.71. Disputed-among-scholars whether ἐγώ εἰμι here is merely affirming the Son-of-God title or consciously-invoking divine-self-identification; majority-evangelical commentary (Bock, Nolland, Marshall) takes the latter reading.

LUK 23:9.notes: SUFFERING-SERVANT SILENCE: Jesus's non-response to Herod echoes ISA 53:7 LXX (ὡς πρόβατον ἐπὶ σφαγὴν ἤχθη, καὶ ὡς ἀμνὸς ἐναντίον τοῦ κείραντος αὐτὸν ἄφωνος οὐκ ἀνοίγει τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ 'as a sheep led to slaughter, and as a lamb silent before its shearer, he opens not his mouth'). Luke applies the silence-motif specifically to the Herod-scene (unique-Lukan detail), whereas Mark 15:4-5 applies Jesus's silence to Pilate. Both gospels are drawing on the same Suffering-Servant-typology in different trial-scenes.

LUK 23:12.notes: KINGS-AND-RULERS-UNITED: Herod-Pilate reconciliation fulfills PSA 2:2 LXX παρέστησαν οἱ βασιλεῖς τῆς γῆς, καὶ οἱ ἄρχοντες συνήχθησαν ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτὸ κατὰ τοῦ κυρίου καὶ κατὰ τοῦ χριστοῦ αὐτοῦ. Luke MAKES THE CITATION EXPLICIT in ACT 4:25-27 where Peter and the early church quote Psalm 2:1-2 and apply it directly to Herod, Pilate, Gentiles, and peoples of Israel gathered against 'your holy servant Jesus.' This is one of the most explicit Luke-Acts OT-fulfillment-interpretations; the LUK 23:12 event is the narrative-anchor for the ACT 4:25-27 interpretation.

LUK 23:22.notes: SUFFERING-SERVANT INNOCENCE: Pilate's third declaration οὐδὲν αἴτιον θανάτου εὗρον ἐν αὐτῷ ('I have found no fault deserving death in him') echoes the Servant-Song's assertion in ISA 53:9 LXX: ἀνομίαν οὐκ ἐποίησεν, οὐδὲ εὑρέθη δόλος ἐν τῷ στόματι αὐτοῦ ('he committed no lawlessness, nor was deceit found in his mouth'). Luke's triple-repetition of this formula (vv.4, 14-15, 22) builds a cumulative-echo of the servant's innocence across the trial-scene. The verb εὑρίσκω 'find' appears in both texts, tying the Roman-judicial finding to the Isaianic prophetic declaration. One of several Isa-53-echoes woven through Luke's passion.

LUK 23:29.notes: BARREN-BLESSED INVERSION: Jesus's 'blessed are the barren' reverses the normal Jewish-blessing of childbearing, echoing HOS 9:14 (δὸς αὐτοῖς, κύριε· τί δώσεις αὐτοῖς; δὸς αὐτοῖς μήτραν ἀτεκνοῦσαν καὶ μαστοὺς ξηρούς 'give them, O LORD — what shall you give? Give them a miscarrying womb and dry breasts') — Hosea's judgment-oracle on idolatrous Israel, re-applied by Jesus to Jerusalem facing the 70 CE siege. During the siege, barrenness becomes mercy (no children to watch starve). Thematically parallels LUK 21:23 'woe to those who are nursing in those days.'

LUK 23:30.notes: DIRECT-QUOTATION: Jesus quotes HOS 10:8 LXX καὶ ἐροῦσιν τοῖς ὄρεσιν Καλύψατε ἡμᾶς, καὶ τοῖς βουνοῖς Πέσατε ἐφ’ ἡμᾶς, with the mountains/hills pairing chiastically-reversed (LUK: mountains-fall + hills-cover; HOS: mountains-cover + hills-fall). Hosea's original-context: northern-kingdom Samaria facing Assyrian-judgment. Jesus applies it to Jerusalem facing Roman-siege. The same citation appears in REV 6:16 at the Day-of-the-Lord — a judgment-motif that becomes standard NT eschatological language. ADDED in editing pass for LUK 23.

LUK 23:31.notes: GREEN-TREE / DRY-TREE JUDGMENT: Jesus's riddle draws on EZK 20:47 LXX (21:3 in LXX numbering): Ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἀνάπτω ἐν σοὶ πῦρ, καὶ καταφάγεται ἐν σοὶ πᾶν ξύλον χλωρὸν καὶ πᾶν ξύλον ξηρόν 'behold I kindle a fire in you, and it shall consume every green tree and every dry tree in you.' Ezekiel's original-oracle: God's judgment-fire consumes both righteous-and-wicked as indiscriminate-wrath. Jesus applies the green/dry-tree contrast a-fortiori: if the green-tree (innocent one, himself) faces this treatment, how much more the dry-tree (Jerusalem)? Some scholars also cite PRO 11:31 LXX (cited at 1 PET 4:18) as a parallel a-fortiori logic.

LUK 23:32.notes: NUMBERED WITH TRANSGRESSORS — NARRATIVE FULFILLMENT: Luke's introduction of the two criminals being led away with Jesus (ἤγοντο δὲ καὶ ἕτεροι κακοῦργοι δύο σὺν αὐτῷ) sets up the narrative-fulfillment of ISA 53:12 LXX (καὶ ἐν τοῖς ἀνόμοις ἐλογίσθη) which Jesus himself cited one chapter earlier at LUK 22:37 (τοῦτο τὸ γεγραμμένον δεῖ τελεσθῆναι ἐν ἐμοί, Τὸ καὶ μετὰ ἀνόμων ἐλογίσθη — 'this scripture must be fulfilled in me, "he was numbered with the transgressors"'). The ἕτεροι κακοῦργοι 'other-of-different-kind' (ἕτεροι NOT ἄλλοι, per uW figs-distinguish) marks Jesus as innocent-but-counted-with the criminals. Dovetails with v.33 (the actual crucifixion alongside them) to complete the fulfillment-scene.

LUK 23:33.notes: CRUCIFIED-BETWEEN-CRIMINALS — ISA 53:12 FULFILLMENT: the placement of the two criminals at Jesus's right and left (ὃν μὲν ἐκ δεξιῶν ὃν δὲ ἐξ ἀριστερῶν) visually fulfills the ISA 53:12 prophecy (καὶ ἐν τοῖς ἀνόμοις ἐλογίσθη — 'numbered with the transgressors') that Jesus cited at LUK 22:37. All four gospels record this positioning (MRK 15:27; MAT 27:38; JHN 19:18). Mark adds an explicit fulfillment-formula (TR/Byz reading at MRK 15:28) quoting Isa 53:12, absent from SBLGNT/NA28 but witnessing early Christian recognition of this scene as Isa-53 fulfillment.

LUK 23:34.notes: DIRECT-QUOTATION (second-half of verse): διαμεριζόμενοι δὲ τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ ἔβαλον κλήρους directly applies PSA 21:19 LXX (22:18 MT): διεμερίσαντο τὰ ἱμάτιά μου ἑαυτοῖς, καὶ ἐπὶ τὸν ἱματισμόν μου ἔβαλον κλῆρον. This is one of the most commonly-cited messianic-fulfillment passages in the NT — appears in ALL FOUR gospels at the crucifixion: MRK 15:24, MAT 27:35, LUK 23:34b, JHN 19:23-24 (John quotes the Psalm explicitly). Psalm 22 is the background-scripture for the entire-crucifixion-scene (vv.35, 36 also echo this Psalm). NOTE ON FIRST-HALF: the saying 'Father forgive them, for they know not what they do' is textually-disputed (absent from 𝔓⁷⁵, ℵ*, B, D, W, Θ); SBLGNT prints with diamond-brackets; see RULES §5 Tier-3 convention (rendered in ⟦...⟧ with extended note in translation JSON).

LUK 23:35.notes: MOCKERY OF THE RIGHTEOUS: Luke's verb ἐκμυκτηρίζω 'sneer-mockingly' is the exact verb in PSA 21:8 LXX (22:7 MT): πάντες οἱ θεωροῦντές με ἐξεμυκτήρισάν με, ἐλάλησαν ἐν χείλεσιν, ἐκίνησαν κεφαλήν ('all who see me mock me, they speak with lips, they shake head'). The Jewish-leaders' taunt also echoes PSA 21:9 LXX (22:8 MT): ἤλπισεν ἐπὶ κύριον· ῥυσάσθω αὐτόν, σωσάτω αὐτόν, ὅτι θέλει αὐτόν ('he trusted in the LORD — let him deliver him, let him save him, for he delights in him'). Luke's composition deliberately layers Psalm-22 language through the mockery-scene, mirroring Mark 15:29 (κινοῦντες τὰς κεφαλάς — head-shaking also from PSA 21:8). Psalm 22 functions as the lectionary-of-the-cross for all four gospels.

LUK 23:36.notes: SOUR-WINE OFFERED TO THE SUFFERER: soldiers offering ὄξος echoes PSA 68:22 LXX (69:21 MT): καὶ ἔδωκαν εἰς τὸ βρῶμά μου χολήν, καὶ εἰς τὴν δίψαν μου ἐπότισάν με ὄξος ('they gave gall for my food, and for my thirst they gave me vinegar'). All four gospels apply this to the cross: MRK 15:36, MAT 27:48, LUK 23:36, JHN 19:29 (explicit ἵνα τελειωθῇ ἡ γραφή). Luke's narrative-sequencing places the vinegar-offering early in the crucifixion-scene as part of three-fold mockery (leaders-soldiers-criminal), whereas Mark/Matt place it near the death and John connects it to Jesus's thirst-cry. ὄξος is technically posca, the Roman-military ration-wine.

LUK 23:44.notes: NOONDAY-DARKNESS AS DAY-OF-THE-LORD: the 3-hour darkness echoes AMO 8:9 LXX καὶ ἔσται ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ, λέγει κύριος ὁ θεός, καὶ δύσεται ὁ ἥλιος μεσημβρίας, καὶ συσκοτάσει ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ἐν ἡμέρᾳ τὸ φῶς ('and it shall be in that day, says the LORD God, and the sun shall go down at noon, and the light shall be darkened on the earth in the day'). Amos's-original-context: Day-of-the-LORD judgment-imagery applied to the northern-kingdom. Luke-Matthew-Mark all preserve the 3-hour darkness (MRK 15:33; MAT 27:45). Meteorologically impossible (Passover = full moon rules out solar eclipse); theologically the day-of-judgment imagery descends on the crucifixion-hour as the Messiah bears the judgment for humanity.

LUK 23:46.notes: DIRECT-QUOTATION of DAVID'S PRAYER: εἰς χεῖράς σου παρατίθεμαι τὸ πνεῦμά μου quotes PSA 30:6 LXX (31:5 MT) verbatim. Jesus prays David's Jewish-evening-prayer (used in Second-Temple Jewish liturgy at the day's-end; continued in the Christian Compline-liturgy). Lukan-editorial-choice: Luke replaces Mark 15:34's Psalm-22:1-cry (ἐλωι ἐλωι λεμα σαβαχθανι 'why have you forsaken me') with Psalm-31's trust-prayer — both psalms are Davidic-laments-ending-in-trust; the traditions are complementary not contradictory, reflecting Jesus's multiple-cries over the 6 hours on the cross. Luke frames Jesus's death as faithful-commendation; Stephen's death in ACT 7:59 echoes this with a Christological-modification (Κύριε Ἰησοῦ δέξαι τὸ πνεῦμά μου).

LUK 23:47.notes: CENTURION CONFESSES JESUS AS δίκαιος: Luke's distinctive rendering (vs. MRK 15:39 / MAT 27:54 'Son of God') uses δίκαιος echoing the servant-song ISA 53:11 LXX: δικαιῶσαι δίκαιον εὖ δουλεύοντα πολλοῖς ('to justify a righteous-one who serves many well'). δίκαιος in Roman-context = legally-innocent; in Jewish-Isaian context = eschatologically-righteous-servant. Luke's theology exploits the double-meaning: a Gentile-centurion declares what all five of Luke's innocence-witnesses have declared (Pilate ×3, the penitent-thief, himself) — Jesus is innocent AND the righteous-servant. Luke's Gentile-centurion pattern foreshadows Cornelius in ACT 10 and the book-of-Acts Gentile-mission trajectory.

LUK 23:49.notes: ACQUAINTANCES STAND AFAR: the specific phrase οἱ γνωστοὶ ... μακρόθεν echoes PSA 87:9 LXX (88:8 MT): ἐμάκρυνας τοὺς γνωστούς μου ἀπ’ ἐμοῦ ('you have removed my acquaintances from me'), using the exact LXX-word γνωστούς. PSA 37:12 LXX (38:11 MT) is a closely-parallel lament: οἱ φίλοι μου καὶ οἱ πλησίον μου ἐξ ἐναντίας μου ἤγγισαν καὶ ἔστησαν. Both psalms are righteous-sufferer-laments where friends abandon the sufferer. Luke's γνωστοὶ αὐτῷ μακρόθεν compresses both Psalms. The Galilean-women are noted specifically here to set up their resurrection-witness role (24:1-10).

LUK 23:52.notes: LEGAL-BACKGROUND (not a quotation): Joseph's request for Jesus's body reflects Jewish-law's same-day burial requirement for executed criminals per DEU 21:22-23 LXX: Ἐὰν δὲ γένηται ἔν τινι ἁμαρτία κρίμα θανάτου, καὶ ἀποθάνῃ, καὶ κρεμάσητε αὐτὸν ἐπὶ ξύλου, οὐ κοιμηθήσεται τὸ σῶμα αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τοῦ ξύλου, ἀλλὰ ταφῇ θάψετε αὐτὸν ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ, ὅτι κεκατηραμένος ὑπὸ θεοῦ πᾶς κρεμάμενος ἐπὶ ξύλου ('if anyone is guilty of a capital crime and is put to death, and you hang him on a tree, his body shall not remain on the tree, but you shall bury him the same day, for anyone hanged on a tree is cursed by God'). Paul cites DEU 21:23 (cursed-hanged-on-tree) in GAL 3:13 applying it to Christ's redemptive-death. Joseph's initiative to request the body before sunset was both pious-observance of Deut 21:22-23 AND an act of honoring Jesus beyond the Roman-custom of leaving bodies to decay.

LUK 23:53.notes: HONORED BURIAL FULFILLS ISA 53:9 LXX: καὶ δώσω τοὺς πονηροὺς ἀντὶ τῆς ταφῆς αὐτοῦ καὶ τοὺς πλουσίους ἀντὶ τοῦ θανάτου αὐτοῦ ('he made his grave with the wicked, and with the rich in his death'). Jesus crucified-between-thieves (vv.32-33 fulfills 'with the wicked') AND buried in a rich-man's unused rock-hewn-tomb (v.53 fulfills 'with the rich in his death'). Matthew 27:57-60 makes Joseph's wealth explicit (πλούσιος ἀπὸ Ἁριμαθαίας); Luke signals wealth-honor indirectly via the fine-linen, the rock-hewn tomb, and the unused-tomb (triple-negative emphasis). Luke's passion-narrative systematically applies ISA 53 — Jesus is the Suffering-Servant at every stage: silence-before-accusers (23:9), numbered-with-transgressors (22:37), honored-burial (23:53).

MAT 1:1.notes: βίβλος γενέσεως verbatim echoes LXX Gen 2:4 (αὕτη ἡ βίβλος γενέσεως οὐρανοῦ καὶ γῆς) and LXX Gen 5:1 (αὕτη ἡ βίβλος γενέσεως ἀνθρώπων). Matthew frames the Jesus-story as a new creation / new Adam / new humanity by borrowing the Septuagintal title-formula. This is the first of many Matthean Genesis-Exodus typological echoes. See also γένεσις at 1:18 (inclusio-ring with the phrase at 1:1).

MAT 1:9.notes: The three-generation span Uzziah → Jotham → Ahaz → Hezekiah in Matthew genealogy roughly covers Isaiah prophetic ministry (Isa 1:1 names all four kings). Not a direct citation at v.9, but a historical-setting note: Matthew embeds the Isaianic prophetic era within the genealogy so that his later Isa 7:14 fulfillment-citation at v.23 stands on narrative-historical ground. Classified as background_allusion since no verbal borrowing at v.9; only ancestral placement of the Isaiah-era kings.

MAT 1:20.notes: Matthew κατ ὄναρ (in a dream) motif — unique to Matthew among the evangelists, used 6x (1:20; 2:12, 13, 19, 22; 27:19) — typologically echoes the patriarch Joseph-of-Egypt, who receives prophetic dreams in Gen 37 (own dreams of preeminence) and interprets dreams in Gen 40-41 (cupbearer-baker; Pharaoh). Both Josephs are: (a) chosen by divine dream-revelation; (b) preservers of the people of God through Egyptian-exile phases; (c) key protector-figures in the Messianic line (OT-Joseph preserves Israel in Egypt via Gen 45; NT-Joseph preserves infant Messiah in Egypt via Matt 2:13-15). Matthew deliberately uses the Greek name Ἰωσήφ for both, heightening the typological echo. No direct verbal citation, but a sustained narrative-typological pattern worth database recording.

MAT 1:21.notes: σώσει τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ ἀπὸ τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν αὐτῶν thematically echoes Ps 129:8 LXX (MT Ps 130:8): καὶ αὐτὸς λυτρώσεται τὸν Ἰσραὴλ ἐκ πασῶν τῶν ἀνομιῶν αὐτοῦ. σώζω and λυτρόω are semantically adjacent (save / redeem); ἁμαρτία and ἀνομία (sin / iniquity) overlap in LXX usage. Matthew recasts the Psalm redemption-promise as programmatic statement of Jesus mission. The angels name-etymology (Ἰησοῦς = YHWH-saves) makes the psalm echo explicit — Jesus IS the one who fulfills this psalm.

MAT 1:23.notes: DIRECT OT CITATION, Matthew first fulfillment-formula quotation (ἵνα πληρωθῇ at v.22). LXX Isa 7:14: ἰδοὺ ἡ παρθένος ἐν γαστρὶ ἕξει καὶ τέξεται υἱόν, καὶ καλέσεις τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ Εμμανουηλ. Matthew matches LXX verbatim except changes 2nd-sg. καλέσεις (you [sg.] will call) to 3rd-pl. καλέσουσιν (they will call) — shift from Ahaz specific naming to community-wide acclamation of Jesus as God-with-us. MT/LXX divergence: Heb. עַלְמָה (almah, young woman of marriageable age, implicitly but not strictly virgin). LXX παρθένος (virgin, technical sense). Matthew cites LXX specifically because παρθένος supports the virginal-conception narrative. INCLUSIO: Ἐμμανουήλ (God with us) at 1:23 forms Christological bookend with Matt 28:20 (ἐγὼ μεθ ὑμῶν εἰμι — I am with you). HAPAX: Ἐμμανουήλ (Matt 1:23 only in NT).

MAT 2:1.notes: Matthew 2:1 sets the stage for the Mic 5:2 citation at 2:6. The notes mention Mic 5:2 as the prophetic background for Jesus's Bethlehem birth — Matthew's primary citation occurs at v.6 where the scribes quote it. This entry documents the forward-reference at v.1 so the drift-detector can track the narrative setup.

MAT 2:6.notes: Composite citation blending Mic 5:2 (Bethlehem as birthplace of Davidic ruler) + 2 Sam 5:2 LXX (God's word to David 'you shall shepherd my people Israel'). Matthew's οὐδαμῶς ἐλαχίστη ('by no means least') INVERTS Mic 5:2 MT/LXX which read 'you are LITTLE among Judah's clans' — scholarly consensus reads this as Matthew's interpretive paraphrase: Bethlehem's former smallness is reversed by the Messiah's birth. Matthew substitutes ἡγούμενος ('ruler') for LXX's ἄρχων, probably blending with 2 Sam 5:2 LXX ἡγούμενον. The shepherd-Israel phrase is 2 Sam 5:2 LXX, not Micah. Matthew creatively splices two Davidic-Bethlehem OT texts.

MAT 2:11.notes: Magi's gold + frankincense echoes LXX Isa 60:6 (φέροντες χρυσίον καὶ λίβανον — nations bringing gold and frankincense to Zion), and the kings-bringing-tribute scene echoes Ps 72:10-11 LXX (kings of Tarshish/Sheba bringing gifts). Matthew does not explicit-cite but invokes the gentile-pilgrimage-to-Messianic-king typology. Myrrh (σμύρνα) has no specific OT parallel but resonates with Song 3:6 and is proleptic of John 19:39 (Jesus's burial-myrrh) — three gifts symbolically: gold=kingship, frankincense=divinity/worship, myrrh=death/burial (later Christian interpretation).

MAT 2:15.notes: CRITICAL TEXT-CRITICAL OBSERVATION: Matthew follows MT (Hebrew) text, NOT LXX. LXX Hos 11:1 reads ἐξ Αἰγύπτου μετεκάλεσα τὰ τέκνα αὐτοῦ ('out of Egypt I summoned his children' — 3rd-pl collective). Matthew writes ἐξ Αἰγύπτου ἐκάλεσα τὸν υἱόν μου ('I called my son' — 1st-sg to single Son). This matches MT בְּנִי (my son, sg.). Matthew requires the singular for his messianic typology: Jesus as individual Son recapitulating Israel's exodus. In Hosea's original context, 'my son' = Israel (collective). Matthew reads this typologically — Jesus as true-Israel Son, recapitulating Israel's Egypt-sojourn and exodus without Israel's subsequent failures. One of the clearest examples of Matthew's selective MT-over-LXX use.

MAT 2:18.notes: Matthew's Greek generally follows LXX-B tradition (κλαυθμὸς + ὀδυρμός doublet) but diverges in minor details. Jer 31:15 OT context: Jeremiah addresses Babylonian-exile-bound Jews at Ramah (deportation staging-ground); Rachel, their matriarch, poetically weeps for her exiled descendants. IMPORTANT LITERARY CONTEXT: Jer 31:16-17 IMMEDIATELY promises restoration ('there is hope for your future... your children shall return') — Matthew cites only the lament verse, but the original context includes implied restoration-promise. Geography: Ramah is in Benjamin territory north of Jerusalem, not in Bethlehem — but Rachel's tomb is traditionally located between them (Gen 35:19), making her the unifying matriarch of both the Bethlehem-massacred boys and the Benjaminite exiles. Matthew's 'fulfillment' links three trauma-typologies: Herod's massacre, Babylonian exile, and (by extension from Hosea 11:1) Egyptian bondage.

MAT 2:19.notes: Matthew's repeated Joseph-dream-revelation pattern (Matt 1:20, 2:12, 2:13, 2:19, 2:22) structurally echoes the dreams-of-Joseph in Gen 37, 40-41. Matthew's Joseph-of-Nazareth receives five dream-revelations that protect the Holy Family, just as Joseph-of-Egypt received/interpreted dreams that protected his family. Not a citation but a typological-structural allusion that Matthew activates through the dream motif. Joseph-Jesus-Egypt triad in Matt 2 consciously recapitulates Genesis-Joseph-Egypt.

MAT 2:20.notes: The angel's reassurance 'those seeking the life of the child have died' (τεθνήκασιν γὰρ οἱ ζητοῦντες τὴν ψυχὴν τοῦ παιδίου) distinctly echoes Exod 4:19 LXX — God's word to Moses in Midian: τεθνήκασιν γὰρ πάντες οἱ ζητοῦντές σου τὴν ψυχήν ('for all those seeking your life are dead'). Near-verbatim formula. Matthew's Joseph-as-new-Moses typology: a prophet-deliverer sheltered in foreign land, summoned back when the tyrant who sought his life has died. Matthew frames Jesus's early life via Mosaic-exodus structural echoes, reinforced by v.15 (out of Egypt I called my son).

MAT 2:23.notes: CRUX: Matthew cites 'the prophets' (PLURAL) — unique in his formula-quotations — without naming a specific OT verse. No single OT verse says 'he will be called a Nazarene.' Scholarly proposals: (1) WORDPLAY with Isa 11:1 — the Hebrew נֵצֶר netzer ('branch/shoot') describing the Davidic messiah emerging from Jesse's stump; Ναζωραῖος ~ netzer etymological pun (majority scholarly view). (2) NAZIRITE connection to Judg 13:5 (Samson 'a Nazirite from the womb'), Num 6 — Jesus as consecrated-one. (3) DESPISED-STATUS: Matthew synthesizes the prophetic witness that the Messiah would come from an insignificant place (Isa 53:2-3, Ps 22:6) — Nazareth qualifies as despised (cf. John 1:46). Matthew's plural 'prophets' accommodates the composite/thematic reading. No single text-match; Matthew signals his interpretive method (a summary fulfillment, not a proof-text citation).

MAT 3:3.notes: DIRECT CITATION of Isa 40:3 LXX, near-verbatim (shared with Mark 1:3, Luke 3:4-6, John 1:23). Isaiah's original context: comfort to Babylon-exilic Judah — a voice proclaiming YHWH's return with his people via a prepared desert-highway. Synoptic-Johannine consensus applies this to John the Baptist as the herald of the LORD's (= Jesus's) coming. CRITICAL CHRISTOLOGICAL MOVE: Isaiah's κύριος = YHWH in Hebrew/MT, but the NT-Synoptic application of this verse to Jesus implicitly identifies Jesus with YHWH — early high-christology embedded in the citation's very use. The MT/LXX punctuation variance ('voice-crying in-wilderness, prepare' vs 'voice-crying, in-wilderness prepare') is resolved in NT usage in favor of the first reading (wilderness modifies voice-crying, which fits John's geographical setting). Matthew's ὁ ῥηθείς ('the-one-spoken-of') formula identifies John AS the fulfillment-figure predicted — structurally identical to Matthew's other formula-quotations.

MAT 3:4.notes: ELIJAH TYPOLOGY. John's wilderness attire (camel-hair garment + leather belt around his waist) near-quotes 2 Kgs 1:8 LXX description of Elijah (ἀνὴρ δασύς καὶ ζώνην δερματίνην περιεζωσμένος τὴν ὀσφύν αὐτοῦ). Matthew preserves Mark's 1:6 use of this allusion and later makes the Elijah-identification explicit (Matt 11:14 'he is Elijah who was to come'; Matt 17:12-13 after Transfiguration 'Elijah has already come... they did to him whatever they wished'). Jewish expectation (Mal 4:5-6) held Elijah would return before the Day of the Lord. John's wilderness-prophet ascetic-typology activates this expectation — he plays Elijah to Jesus's Messiah. Also antitypal echo of Zech 13:4 (false prophets who do NOT wear hairy garment) — John's authentic prophetic dress contrasts deceivers.

MAT 3:7.notes: John's insult γεννήματα ἐχιδνῶν ('brood of vipers') activates two OT backgrounds: (1) GEN 3 serpent — seed-of-serpent imagery (Gen 3:15 'enmity between your seed and her seed'), morally associating the Pharisees/Sadducees with the primordial deceiver; (2) ISA 59:5 'they hatch viper's eggs' — prophetic indictment of wicked Israel as producing poisonous offspring. Matthew particularly favors this insult (3:7, 12:34, 23:33) — a distinctive Matthean stock-phrase linking John's, Jesus's, and implicitly Satan's-seed imagery (cf. 13:38 'weeds are children of the evil one'). Not a formal citation; thematic/imagistic connection.

MAT 3:9.notes: John's 'God can raise up children for Abraham from these stones' thematically invokes Isa 51:1-2 LXX, where Israel is commanded 'look to the rock from which you were hewn... look to Abraham your father.' John reverses the sense ironically: Abraham's true seed is not guaranteed by ethnic descent but by God's generative power — lithic-to-familial creation echoes the Abrahamic covenant's gift-nature. Possible Aramaic wordplay between בְּנַיָא (benayā 'sons') + אַבְנַיָא (abnayā 'stones') — John may have delivered this in Aramaic, preserved semantically in Greek. The theology (ethnic privilege does not guarantee covenant inclusion) anticipates Pauline arguments in Rom 4, Gal 3-4.

MAT 3:10.notes: Axe-at-root judgment imagery evokes Isa 10:33-34 (God as tree-feller against arrogant Assyria — LXX πρὸς τὴν ῥίζαν κόπτων) and Ezek 31 (Assyria as cedar felled by foreigners). Also Dan 4 (Nebuchadnezzar's felled tree by divine decree). John adapts the OT tree-judgment motif to personal/eschatological individual-accountability — the tree is not a nation but any unfruitful person. This imagery recurs in Matthew's own formulations at 7:19 (identical words) and 13:30 (weeds-wheat) — Matthean echo pattern. The fire-destination at the end of the verse compounds with the OT judgment-by-fire tradition (Isa 66:24, Zeph 1:18).

MAT 3:11.notes: PNEUMATOLOGICAL-ESCHATOLOGICAL COMPOSITE. John's prophecy that the Messiah 'will baptize with the Holy Spirit and fire' invokes three OT texts: (1) JOEL 2:28-29 (LXX 3:1-2): eschatological Spirit-outpouring on all flesh — programmatic for Pentecost (Acts 2:16-21); (2) MAL 3:2-3: Messiah as refiner's fire who purifies Levi's sons — purification-by-fire; (3) ISA 4:4: 'spirit of judgment and spirit of burning' — God's cleansing-fire for Zion. Matthew+Luke (not Mark) preserve the 'and fire' component. INTERPRETIVE AMBIGUITY: fire as (a) purification-refining for believers, (b) judgment-consuming for the unrepentant, or (c) further description of the Spirit itself. Most likely (a)+(b) — dual-edge eschatological reality. NT fulfillment: Acts 2 (tongues-as-fire Spirit-outpouring) is the positive edge; final judgment-fire (Rev 20) is the negative edge.

MAT 3:12.notes: AGRICULTURAL-JUDGMENT IMAGERY. John's winnowing-fork / threshing-floor / wheat-barn / chaff-fire metaphor draws on: (1) PSA 1:4: 'the wicked are like chaff that the wind drives away' — foundational wisdom-Psalm chaff-imagery; (2) ISA 41:15-16: God makes Israel a threshing-sledge that pulverizes mountains (judgment via threshing); (3) MAL 4:1: Day of the LORD burns the arrogant as stubble in fire. John adapts for eschatological separation — the coming-one will thresh/sort humanity. Matthew's own later developments: 13:30 (parable of weeds-among-wheat with similar barn/burn imagery), 13:40-42 (final judgment explicit), 25:31-46 (sheep-goats sorting). The 'unquenchable fire' (ἄσβεστος, also at Mark 9:43-48) = eternal judgment, echoing Isa 66:24.

MAT 3:15.notes: Jesus's 'to fulfill all righteousness' invokes OT righteousness-theology: (1) ISA 53:11 LXX 'the righteous one, my servant, will justify many' — the Suffering Servant as righteousness-accomplisher for others; (2) ZEC 9:9 'your king comes to you, righteous and victorious' — the Messianic king bringing righteousness. Jesus's baptism-in-solidarity with sinners previews his substitutionary-righteousness project (Matt 27:4 'I betrayed innocent blood' + Matt 27:19 'righteous man' + Matt 27:24 'I am innocent of this righteous man's blood'). Matthean δικαιοσύνη is a LOADED term throughout his gospel (Matt 5:6, 5:10, 5:20, 6:1, 6:33, 21:32) — Jesus here sets the trajectory of identifying with sinners to bring them into right-standing. Matthew-distinctive saying; unique to Matthew among synoptics.

MAT 3:16.notes: SPIRIT+WATER+DOVE NEW-CREATION IMAGERY. The Spirit descending 'like a dove' over waters at Jesus's baptism echoes: (1) GEN 1:2 'the Spirit of God was hovering [MT מרחפת, LXX ἐπεφέρετο] over the waters' — creation-inauguration; Jesus's baptism is new-creation-inauguration; (2) GEN 8:8-12 Noah's dove + post-flood new-beginning — dove as sign of God's favor after judgment. Also possibly Song 2:14 (dove as beloved imagery). Patristic tradition (Tertullian, Origen) developed these echoes explicitly. Matthew's Gen-1 parallel invites theological reading: Jesus inaugurates a new creation, indwelt by the same creator-Spirit that hovered at cosmogenesis. The ἀνεῴχθησαν ('were opened') at Matt 3:16 vs Mark's σχιζομένους ('torn open' at Mark 1:10) also echoes Ezek 1:1 (Ezekiel 'heavens were opened' for divine vision).

MAT 3:17.notes: FOUNDATIONAL CHRISTOLOGICAL CITATION. Father's voice from heaven combines three OT Son/Beloved texts: (1) PSA 2:7 LXX 'ὁ υἱός μου εἶ σύ' ('you are my son') — royal-Messianic enthronement Psalm, Davidic-king-as-son-of-God declaration; (2) ISA 42:1 LXX 'ὁ ἀγαπητός μου ... εἰς ὃν εὐδόκησεν ἡ ψυχή μου' ('my beloved... in whom my soul delighted') — Suffering Servant song; (3) GEN 22:2 LXX ὁ υἱός σου ὁ ἀγαπητός (Abraham's 'beloved son' Isaac — binding-near-sacrifice echo). Matthew's voice-saying FUSES these: Jesus is simultaneously royal-Messiah (Ps 2), Suffering Servant (Isa 42), AND beloved-sacrifice-son (Gen 22 Isaac-typology pointing to crucifixion). One sentence carrying three OT identities — Matthew's densest Christological compression. SYNOPTIC-DIFFERENCE: Matthew's 3rd-person 'this is' (public declaration) vs Mark/Luke's 2nd-person 'you are' (addressed to Jesus). Matthew pairs this with 17:5 Transfiguration (same Father-voice formula + 'listen to him!') bracketing Jesus's ministry. The εὐδοκέω verb echoes Isa 42:1 LXX — Matthew's text-critical preference here matches LXX wording closely. For the LXX-MT comparison: LXX Isa 42:1 reads ὁ ἐκλεκτός μου, προσεδέξατο αὐτὸν ἡ ψυχή μου — Matthew's substitution of ὁ ἀγαπητός and εὐδόκησα shows his compositional reworking.

MAT 4:1.notes: Wilderness-testing typology: 40-days echoes Israel's 40-years (Deut 8:2-3), Moses's 40-days on Sinai (Exod 34:28), Elijah's 40-day journey to Horeb (1 Kgs 19:8). Jesus as new-Israel/new-Moses retracing the wilderness path to succeed where Israel failed.

MAT 4:2.notes: 40-day-40-night number echo: Moses on Sinai (Exod 24:18 + Deut 9:18 second 40-day fast for Israel's golden-calf sin), Elijah's journey to Horeb (1 Kgs 19:8). Matthew uniquely restores 'forty nights' (Mark 1:13 omits) to match Mosaic formula. Signals Jesus as new-Moses in wilderness-fasting preparation.

MAT 4:3.notes: Wilderness-manna testing typology: Israel demanded bread on their own terms (Exod 16, Num 11). Satan tempts Jesus to take the same shortcut — use divine power to satisfy physical appetite on demand. Jesus refuses, setting up the Deut 8:3 citation at v.4.

MAT 4:4.notes: Near-verbatim LXX quotation ('παντὶ ῥήματι ἐκπορευομένῳ διὰ στόματος θεοῦ'). MT has parallel sense but different vocabulary ('everything that proceeds out of the mouth of the LORD'). Context: Moses reminds Israel that God led them through 40 years of wilderness-hunger to teach dependence on God's word, not on bread. Jesus places himself in Israel's position but succeeds where Israel failed.

MAT 4:6.notes: Satan's selective quotation: Ps 91:11a + 12 but OMITS 'ἐν πάσαις ταῖς ὁδοῖς σου' ('in all your ways') from v.11b. This ellipsis turns a covenant-conditional promise (protection while walking in God's ways) into an unconditional invitation to test God. Patristic and Reformation commentary: 'The devil knows Scripture but mangles it.'

MAT 4:7.notes: Verbatim LXX quotation ('Οὐκ ἐκπειράσεις κύριον τὸν θεόν σου'). Context: 'Do not test the LORD your God as you tested him at Massah' — referring to Exod 17:1-7 where Israel demanded water. Jesus turns Satan's invitation inside-out: jumping-to-be-rescued would itself be Massah-testing. Scripture-against-Scripture hermeneutic against Satan's Ps 91 misuse.

MAT 4:8.notes: Moses on Mount Nebo inversion: God showed Moses the Promised Land from Nebo's summit as covenantal-inheritance. Satan inverts the scene — worldly kingdoms (not God's inheritance), devil as grantor (not God). Geographical impossibility of seeing 'all kingdoms' from physical peak signals visionary/apocalyptic-transport (cf. 4 Ezra, 2 Baruch mountain-vision motifs).

MAT 4:9.notes: First-commandment violation frame: Satan's demand 'fall down and worship me' directly targets 'You shall have no other gods before me' (Decalogue first commandment, Exod 20:3 / Deut 5:7). Jesus's v.10 response (Deut 6:13) cites the Deuteronomic expansion of this commandment.

MAT 4:10.notes: LXX: 'κύριον τὸν θεόν σου φοβηθήσῃ καὶ αὐτῷ λατρεύσεις.' Matthew/Jesus substitutes προσκυνήσεις (worship) for φοβηθήσῃ (fear) AND adds μόνῳ (only) — sharpening to first-commandment exclusivity. Luke 4:8 has identical form, suggesting pre-Matthean Jesus-tradition or common source. Jesus's three Deut citations (4:4=8:3, 4:7=6:16, 4:10=6:13) all from Moses's covenant-renewal at Moab — the central OT text for Israel's obedience.

MAT 4:11.notes: Elijah-in-wilderness angelic-provision typology: angel brings bread+water to sustain Elijah after 40-day fast at Horeb. Satan offered bread-by-self-power (4:3) and was refused; now angels bring the needed provision. Pattern: obedience-precedes-provision, not provision-by-presumption. Also echoes Mark 1:13 narrative-closure.

MAT 4:12.notes: Matthew's narrative preparation for the ISA 9:1-2 fulfillment citation at vv.15-16. Jesus's withdrawal to Galilee sets up the geographic-fulfillment. Parallel παραδίδωμι verb (John 'handed over') structurally links John's fate to Jesus's (same verb at Matt 26:15,21,etc.). No direct OT citation but thematic-typological setup.

MAT 4:13.notes: Matthew's geographic relocation to Capernaum in Zebulun/Naphtali territory intentionally prepares the Isa 9:1 citation at v.15. The twin mentions of Zebulun + Naphtali (historically first territories to fall to Assyria, 2 Kgs 15:29) frame Jesus's arrival there as the light-in-darkness promise fulfillment. The move is narrated first, then prophetically interpreted.

MAT 4:15.notes: Direct citation opening vv.15-16 Isaiah-block. Matthew's structure close to LXX but rearranged: LXX has 'region of Zebulun, land of Naphtali' in reversed order with different connectives. Context of Isa 8-9: Assyrian crisis of 734-732 BCE when Tiglath-Pileser III annexed Israel's northern territories (Zebulun, Naphtali) — first Israelite land to fall. Isaiah's light-in-darkness prophecy promises this darkest area will receive God's restoration first.

MAT 4:16.notes: Completes vv.15-16 Isaiah-citation block. Isa 9:1 (English) = Isa 8:23 (Hebrew BHS) and Isa 9:2 (English) = Isa 9:1 (Hebrew BHS) — verse-numbering differs. Light-metaphor anticipates John 8:12 ('I am the light of the world') and John 1:4-5 ('light shines in darkness'). 'Shadow of death' parallels Ps 23:4 imagery. Matthew's use grounds Jesus's Galilean ministry in Messianic-restoration texts.

MAT 4:17.notes: Apocalyptic kingdom-imminence tradition: Dan 2:44 ('God of heaven will set up a kingdom which shall never be destroyed') + Dan 7:14 ('to him was given dominion and glory and kingdom'). Matthew's signature phrase 'kingdom of the heavens' (~32x) likely reflects Jewish-pious circumlocution for divine name via 'heavens' metonymy. MAT 3:2 (John's call) + MAT 4:17 (Jesus's call) form Matthean inclusio — Jesus takes up where John left off with the SAME exact call.

MAT 4:19.notes: Fishers-of-men metaphor: Jer 16:16 has 'many fishermen' as image of negative-judgment (God sending fishers to catch Israel for exile). Ezek 47:9-10 features fishermen at eschatological-river. Matthew/Jesus re-appropriates the OT fisher-imagery POSITIVELY as kingdom-ingathering. Transformation of OT-judgment-motif into Gospel-salvation-motif.

MAT 4:23.notes: Eschatological-healing as kingdom-signal: Isa 35:5-6 (blind-see, deaf-hear, lame-leap, mute-sing) is the paradigmatic OT text for Messianic age's healing-signs. Matthew's healing-summary at 4:23 implicitly invokes this Isaianic-promise. Jesus later explicitly cites it when answering John's disciples (Matt 11:4-5). Healing as kingdom-sign = Isaianic fulfillment.

MAT 5:1.notes: Jesus ascends 'the mountain' to deliver new-covenant teaching — Matthew positions him as new-Moses giving Torah-of-the-kingdom. Mountain-ascent-to-teach echoes Moses at Sinai (Exod 19:3 καὶ Μωυσῆς ἀνέβη εἰς τὸ ὄρος τοῦ θεοῦ; Exod 24:12 ἀνάβηθι πρός με εἰς τὸ ὄρος). Matthean structural framing — Matthew will pair this Sermon-on-Mountain with the Olivet Discourse (ch.24-25) and Great Commission from a mountain (28:16).

MAT 5:3.notes: Isa 61:1 LXX εὐαγγελίσασθαι πτωχοῖς — Messianic-mission gospel-to-poor which Jesus programmatically claims at Luke 4:18-19. Matthew 5:3 πτωχοὶ τῷ πνεύματι refines 'poor' to inner-dependence. Also Psa 34:18 LXX ἐγγὺς κύριος τοῖς συντετριμμένοις τὴν καρδίαν (Lord-near-the-contrite-heart). First Beatitude frames whole Sermon through Isaianic-servant-anointing theme.

MAT 5:4.notes: Isa 61:1-3 LXX is Matthew's Beatitudes-blueprint: παρακαλέσαι πάντας τοὺς πενθοῦντας (to comfort all who mourn) — exact-lexeme-match (πενθέω + παρακαλέω). Isa 61 is the Messianic-program Jesus programmatically claims (Luke 4:18). Second Beatitude explicitly activates the anointed-comforter frame.

MAT 5:5.notes: Near-verbatim Ps 37:11 LXX 'οἱ δὲ πραεῖς κληρονομήσουσιν γῆν' — Matthew preserves exactly, adding articular τὴν. Ps 37 is a wisdom-psalm on patient-waiting vs wicked-flourishing: 'fret not... the meek will inherit the land... evildoers shall be cut off.' Jesus transfers Israelite-land-promise to eschatological-renewed-earth inheritance (cf. Rom 4:13 Abraham heir of kosmos).

MAT 5:6.notes: Isa 55:1-2 LXX 'come to the waters' invitation + Ps 107:9 LXX 'the hungry soul he filled with good' (ψυχὴν πεινῶσαν ἐνέπλησεν ἀγαθῶν) supply the hunger-thirst-satisfied imagery. Matthew adds 'for righteousness' sharpening Isaianic-banquet-invitation to covenantal-faithfulness-desire. Fourth Beatitude middle of 8-Beatitude block.

MAT 5:8.notes: Ps 24:3-4 LXX 'τίς ἀναβήσεται εἰς τὸ ὄρος τοῦ κυρίου... ἀθῷος χερσὶν καὶ καθαρὸς τῇ καρδίᾳ' (who ascends the mountain of the Lord... clean-hands-and-pure-heart). Matthew echoes καθαρὸς τῇ καρδίᾳ exactly. Temple-ascent liturgical context reapplied: the pure-hearted see God (beatific-vision promise). Also Job 19:26-27; 1 John 3:2; Rev 22:4 for eschatological vision-of-God.

MAT 5:12.notes: Prophets-persecuted historical-pattern: 2 Chr 36:16 'mocking the messengers of God'; Neh 9:26 'killed your prophets who had warned them'; Jer 2:30 'your own sword has devoured your prophets'. Matthew develops this Matthean-through-line heavily at 23:29-37 (woes against persecutors-of-prophets). Sufferings of disciples = continuation of prophetic vocation.

MAT 5:16.notes: Isa 60:1-3 LXX nations-coming-to-Zion's-light-to-glorify-God pattern; Isa 49:6 Servant-as-φῶς ἐθνῶν (light-of-nations). Matthew applies Isaianic-light-theology both to Jesus (Matt 4:15-16 citing Isa 9:1-2) and here derivatively to disciples. Good-works-leading-to-glorify-God = missional-theology grounded in Servant-songs tradition.

MAT 5:18.notes: Permanence-of-God's-word tradition: Isa 40:8 LXX 'τὸ δὲ ῥῆμα τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα' (word-of-our-God-stands-forever). Ps 119:89 LXX 'εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα κύριε ὁ λόγος σου διαμένει' (forever-Lord-your-word-remains). Matthew extends Torah-permanence until-all-fulfilled. Cosmic heaven-and-earth-will-pass frame also echoes Mark 13:31 where the same passes-away-but-word-remains contrast applies.

MAT 5:21.notes: DIRECT citation of Exod 20:13 LXX 'οὐ φονεύσεις' (Sixth Commandment) = Deut 5:17 LXX (identical). Matthew's Jesus uses this as starting point for the first antithesis, revealing the inner intent (not-murder-only-externally but not-angry-internally). Torah-base preserved, interpretation extended.  ||  Summary-citation of Torah judicial-consequences for murder: Gen 9:6 'whoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shed'; Lev 24:17 'whoever takes a human life shall be put to death'; Num 35:30-31 witness-evidence + no-ransom for murderer. Matthew's 'whoever murders will be subject to judgment' summarizes this corpus without single-verse-anchor.

MAT 5:23.notes: Mercy-over-sacrifice tradition: Hos 6:6 LXX 'ἔλεος θέλω καὶ οὐ θυσίαν' (Matthew cites this directly at 9:13 and 12:7); 1 Sam 15:22 'to obey is better than sacrifice'; Ps 51:16-17; Isa 1:11-17. Rabbinic tradition (Mishnah Yoma 8:9) preserves same priority: reconciliation-with-neighbor must precede temple-sacrifice.

MAT 5:27.notes: DIRECT citation of Exod 20:14 LXX 'οὐ μοιχεύσεις' (Seventh Commandment) = Deut 5:18 LXX (identical). Second antithesis — Jesus moves from external-act (adultery-committed) to internal-disposition (lust-in-heart). The Commandment-7 + Commandment-10 (covet-neighbor's-wife Exod 20:17) are interpretively-linked via shared ἐπιθυμέω-vocabulary.

MAT 5:31.notes: Summary-citation of Deut 24:1-4 LXX — the divorce-certificate provision. Matthew's 'Whoever divorces his wife, let him give her a certificate of divorce' paraphrases rather than quotes-verbatim. OT-function: protect divorced-wife by formalizing her marriageable-again status. Jesus acknowledges the provision but tightens to exception-only (cf. Matt 19:7-9 explicit discussion of why-Moses-permitted-divorce = hardness-of-hearts).

MAT 5:33.notes: Summary-citation of Torah oath-integrity corpus: Lev 19:12 LXX 'καὶ οὐκ ὀμεῖσθε τῷ ὀνόματί μου ἐπ' ἀδίκῳ' (don't swear-by-my-name-falsely); Num 30:2 LXX 'when a man vows a vow to the Lord... he shall not break his word'; Deut 23:21-23 LXX 'when you vow, do not delay to fulfill it.' Matthew's abbreviated form covers both false-swearing and oath-non-fulfillment.

MAT 5:34.notes: Isa 66:1 LXX 'ὁ οὐρανός μοι θρόνος ἡ δὲ γῆ ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν μου' (heaven-my-throne earth-my-footstool). Matthew distributes across vv.34 (heaven=throne) + v.35 (earth=footstool). Acts 7:49 quotes same Isa 66:1 in Stephen's speech. Jesus's argument: any sworn-by entity ultimately is God-owned, so oath-casuistry that distinguishes 'by-heaven' (not-binding) from 'by-God' (binding) fails.

MAT 5:35.notes: Isa 66:1 LXX (earth-footstool continues from v.34) + Ps 48:2 LXX (3:3 in LXX-numbering) 'ἡ πόλις τοῦ βασιλέως τοῦ μεγάλου' (city-of-the-great-king). Psalm 48 is a Zion-song celebrating Jerusalem as God's dwelling. Matthew's Jesus: every sworn-by entity (heaven/earth/Jerusalem/one's-own-head v.36) is ultimately God-connected, rendering oath-distinctions meaningless.

MAT 5:38.notes: DIRECT composite citation of lex talionis from three Torah-codes: Exod 21:24 LXX (Covenant Code), Lev 24:20 LXX (Holiness Code), Deut 19:21 LXX (Deuteronomic Code). Ancient Near Eastern parallel Code of Hammurabi §§196-201. OT function: judicial-proportionality limiting revenge (not permitting personal-vengeance). Jesus addresses the popular misreading that made it a revenge-license.

MAT 5:39.notes: OT non-retaliation wisdom-partial-precedent: Prov 20:22 LXX 'do not say, I will repay the evil'; Lam 3:30 LXX 'he should give his cheek to the one who strikes him, he should be filled with reproach.' Jesus radicalizes: turn-the-other-cheek = active-engagement, not mere-passivity. Romans 12:17-21 develops same non-retaliation ethic. 1 Pet 2:23 models on Christ's passion-non-retaliation.

MAT 5:42.notes: Torah-generous-lending tradition: Deut 15:7-11 LXX 'do not harden your heart... open your hand freely to your poor brother'; Ps 37:26 LXX 'all day long shows mercy and lends.' Jewish wisdom-tradition Sir 29:1-2 reinforces. Jesus's teaching continues and intensifies the generous-disposition ethic.

MAT 5:43.notes: DIRECT citation of Lev 19:18 LXX 'ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν' — Matthew truncates (drops 'as yourself'). Lev 19:18 is the key OT love-command, cited by Jesus multiple times as second-greatest-commandment (Matt 22:39). Matthew 5:43 uses the truncated form because v.43b's 'hate your enemy' is not quoting OT but popular-extrapolation that Jesus will challenge.  ||  'Hate your enemy' is NOT in the OT as a commandment. It may draw on passages like Deut 7:2 (don't make covenants with Canaanites), Deut 23:3-6 (exclude Moabites/Ammonites), or Ps 139:21-22 (I hate those who hate you). Qumran Community Rule 1QS 1:9-11 explicitly teaches love-sons-of-light, hate-sons-of-darkness. Jesus challenges this sectarian/extrapolated interpretation as going-beyond-Torah-intent. The antithesis here is NOT Jesus-vs-Torah but Jesus-vs-interpretive-tradition.

MAT 5:44.notes: Prov 25:21-22 LXX 'if your enemy is hungry, feed him' = partial OT-precedent for enemy-kindness (paraphrased at Rom 12:20). Lam 3:30 turn-cheek + Prov 25 feed-enemy together form OT thematic-partial-precedent. But Jesus's 'love your enemies' is categorically-more-radical — no explicit OT commandment to LOVE enemies (only 'help' them in specific limited-instances). Matthean radicalization beyond OT.

MAT 5:45.notes: Common-grace-tradition: Job 25:3 'can his armies be numbered? Upon whom does his light not rise?' — universal divine-light. Ps 145:9 LXX 'χρηστὸς κύριος τοῖς σύμπασιν' (Lord-good-to-all); 'his mercies are over all his works.' Eccl 9:2 'same event happens to all — to righteous and wicked.' Jewish wisdom-tradition: God's-universal-provision grounds human-universal-love. Greco-Roman philosophical parallel Seneca De Beneficiis 4.26.

MAT 5:48.notes: Imitation-of-God ethics: Lev 19:2 LXX 'ἅγιοι ἔσεσθε ὅτι ἅγιος ἐγὼ κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν' (be holy for I-Lord-your-God am holy); Deut 18:13 LXX 'τέλειος ἔσῃ ἐναντίον κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ σου' (be-τέλειος before Lord-your-God). Matthew echoes the Deut-18:13 future-indicative-imperative + τέλειος-language. Lev 19 is the Holiness Code whose love-neighbor-as-self command Jesus has been applying throughout. Luke 6:36 parallel renders 'merciful' (οἰκτίρμων) making explicit the mercy-dimension of perfection-as-love-completeness.

MAT 6:8.notes: Isa 65:24 LXX 'before they call I will answer' — foundational prophetic text for pre-emptive divine knowledge of human need.

MAT 6:9.notes: Ezek 36:23 LXX 'I will sanctify my great name' — eschatological-vindication of divine name. Isa 29:23 'sanctify the Holy One of Jacob.' 1 Chr 29:10 LXX 'blessed are you LORD God of Israel our Father' — Davidic prayer. Lord's Prayer opening echoes these threads: divine-fatherhood + name-sanctification.

MAT 6:10.notes: Ps 103:20-21 LXX angels doing God's will perfectly — heavenly-pattern of will-done. 1 Chr 29:11-12 LXX 'yours LORD the kingdom' — source of the Byzantine-doxology-addition at Matt 6:13.

MAT 6:11.notes: Exod 16:4 LXX wilderness-manna 'daily-to-daily' provision pattern. Prov 30:8 LXX ta deonta kai ta autarke — Agur's prayer for enough-food-not-too-much-not-too-little. epiousios may echo Prov 30:8 thematic-spirit if not lexical.

MAT 6:12.notes: Ps 32:1-5 LXX 'blessed are those whose iniquities are forgiven; divine-forgiveness-as-blessedness. Lev 19:18 LXX love-neighbor as foundation of forgiving-neighbor. Matthew's financial-metaphor 'debts' uniquely captures Aramaic hoba = both-debt-and-sin (not Luke's hamartias).

MAT 6:13.notes: Deut 6:16 LXX 'don't test LORD your God' (Matt 4:7 already cited). Deut 13:3 'LORD your God is testing you to know your heart.' Byzantine-doxology addition draws on 1 Chr 29:11-13 LXX — Davidic doxology. Bracketed doxology preserved for liturgical-tradition continuity.

MAT 6:14.notes: Lev 19:18 LXX love-neighbor as foundation of bilateral-forgiveness-ethic. Ps 103:10-12 LXX 'he has not dealt with us according to our iniquities' + 'as east is from west he has removed our transgressions' — divine-mercy pattern disciples imitate horizontally.

MAT 6:15.notes: Prov 21:13 LXX 'one who refuses to hear the poor will not be heard when he cries.' Retributive-reciprocity structure parallels Matt 6:15 refusal-to-forgive-invites-non-forgiveness.

MAT 6:16.notes: Isa 58:3-5 critiques superficial-fasting: 'Why have we fasted and you have not seen?...you fast only to quarrel.' Joel 2:12-13 'rend your hearts not your garments' — inner-over-outer. Jesus's critique aligns with prophetic-tradition.

MAT 6:19.notes: Prov 23:4-5 LXX 'don't exhaust yourself becoming rich... [wealth] will fly away like an eagle.' Wealth-flies-away wisdom-theme directly supporting Matthew's moth-rust-thief imagery of earthly-treasure-transience.

MAT 6:20.notes: Ps 119:111 LXX 'I have inherited your testimonies forever' — eternal-inheritance theme. Prov 15:16 LXX 'better a little with fear of LORD than great treasure with trouble' — quality-over-quantity wisdom grounding 'heavenly treasure' superiority.

MAT 6:21.notes: Prov 4:23 LXX 'with all diligence guard your heart, for from it come the outflowings of life' — heart-as-life-center wisdom-principle. Matthew's 'where treasure is, heart will be' is inverted-mirror: treasure-direction shows heart-orientation.

MAT 6:22.notes: Prov 20:27 LXX 'light of the LORD is human breath/spirit' — inner-faculty as light. MT 'lamp of YHWH the breath of man' closer to Matthean lychnos imagery. Wisdom-tradition of inner-light-faculty.

MAT 6:23.notes: Deut 15:9 LXX warns against 'your eye being evil-hostile toward the poor brother in sabbatical-year debt-release.' Evil-eye Hebraism for stingy-selfish. Prov 28:22 LXX 'the stingy-evil-eyed man hastens to be rich.' Context favors stinginess-reading of poneros ophthalmos.

MAT 6:24.notes: Josh 24:14-15 LXX 'choose this day whom you will serve' — covenantal-choice. Deut 6:5 LXX Shema 'love LORD your God with all your heart' — undivided-heart-love. Matthew's two-masters saying applies covenant-exclusivity to money-as-alternative-god.

MAT 6:25.notes: Ps 55:22 LXX 'cast upon the LORD your anxiety' — merimna shared-lexeme with Matt 6:25-34 merimnao verb-family. 1 Pet 5:7 directly quotes this Psalm; Matt 6:25 shares theological-thread of anxiety-cast-upon-providential-God.

MAT 6:26.notes: Ps 147:9 LXX 'gives to the cattle their food and to the young ravens that call upon him.' Job 38:41 'who provides for the raven its prey when its young cry to God?' Luke 12:24 makes the raven-link explicit; Matthew generalizes to 'birds of heaven' but same OT-thematic-substrate.

MAT 6:28.notes: Isa 40:6-8 LXX 'all flesh is grass, all human glory like flower of grass; grass withers, flower falls.' Matthew inverts Isaianic lament-of-human-transience into providence-argument: if God clothes transient-flowers, he will certainly clothe you.

MAT 6:29.notes: 1 Kgs 10:4-7 LXX + 2 Chr 9:3-6 LXX: Queen of Sheba's visit to Solomon, overwhelmed by his royal-splendor. Solomon as paradigm of maximum human-royal-magnificence — even Solomon's peak-glory cannot match natural-beauty of field-flowers.

MAT 6:30.notes: Continuation of Isa 40:6-8 thread — grass-today-furnace-tomorrow. Ps 90:5-6 LXX 'morning like grass passes, morning flowers and passes' — human-transience via grass-imagery. Jesus applies to providence-argument.

MAT 6:32.notes: Ps 139:1-4 LXX 'LORD you have searched me and known me' + knows-thoughts-before-spoken. Divine-omniscience of need precedes prayer-request.

MAT 6:33.notes: 1 Kgs 3:11-13 LXX Solomon asks for wisdom and receives wealth+honor+long life as bonus — 'seek-first-God's-wisdom-material-things-added' pattern. Prov 3:9-10 LXX 'honor LORD with firstfruits... storerooms filled with grain.' Ps 37:4 LXX 'delight in LORD, he will give heart's requests.'

MAT 6:34.notes: Prov 27:1 LXX 'don't boast about tomorrow, for you don't know what tomorrow will bring.' Wisdom-proverb on tomorrow-epistemic-humility directly underlies Matthew's 'don't worry about tomorrow.' Note epiousa 'coming day' cognate-root with epiousios at Matt 6:11.

MAT 7:1.notes: Lev 19:15 LXX requires righteous-judgment without partiality. Jesus's 'do not judge' in context opposes harsh/improper judging, aligned with Torah's judicial-fairness ethic.

MAT 7:2.notes: Isa 65:7 LXX has ἀνταποδώσω (repay) for measure-returned-principle. Mishnah Sotah 1:7 verbatim parallel 'middah k-neged middah' (measure-against-measure) is rabbinic formulation Jesus absorbs. Lex-talionis-judicial-reciprocity rooted in Deut 19:21.

MAT 7:3.notes: Psa 19:12 LXX (psalmist's plea about hidden-faults) — self-examination-principle that undergirds the speck/beam hyperbole. Rabbinic-parallel in b. Arachin 16b R. Tarphon's splinter/beam saying.

MAT 7:6.notes: Prov 9:7-8 LXX 'do not correct the scoffer lest he hate you' underlies the don't-cast-pearls logic. Prov 26:11 LXX 'as-a-dog-returns-to-its-vomit' associates dogs with uncleanness/folly. Lev 11 categorizes both dogs and swine as ritually-unclean animals (pigs cf. Lev 11:7-8; dogs by association).

MAT 7:7.notes: Jer 29:13 LXX 'when-you-seek-me-with-all-your-heart, you-shall-find-me' prime-text for seek/find-pair. Prov 8:17 LXX 'those-who-seek-me-find-me' similar wisdom-invitation.

MAT 7:11.notes: Psa 84:11 LXX (83:12 LXX) 'no good thing will he withhold from those who walk uprightly' for divine-giving-of-good. Isa 49:15 LXX rhetorical 'can-a-mother-forget-her-nursing-child' underlies parental-analogy. Kal-va-chomer (lesser-to-greater) is classical rabbinic argumentation.

MAT 7:12.notes: GOLDEN RULE summary-formula. Positive-form distinct from negative-formulations of Tobit 4:15 LXX (ὃ μισεῖς μηδενὶ ποιήσῃς) and Hillel b.Shabbat 31a. Jesus's positive requires proactive-good, not mere restraint. 'Law-and-the-Prophets' = Hebrew-canon; Matt 22:40 parallel explicitly hangs law-and-prophets on Deut 6:5 + Lev 19:18 love-commandments.

MAT 7:13.notes: Two-ways doctrine foundational OT wisdom. Deut 30:19 LXX 'life-and-death I-have-set-before-you, choose life' covenantal-decision; Jer 21:8 LXX 'way-of-life and way-of-death'; Ps 1:6 LXX 'way-of-righteous/way-of-wicked.' Also prominent in Qumran 1QS 3-4 and early-church-Didache 1-6.

MAT 7:14.notes: Prov 4:18-19 LXX path-of-righteous vs way-of-darkness; Prov 14:12 LXX 'there-is-a-way-that-seems-right but its-end-is-the-ways-of-death' underscoring few-find-narrow-way.

MAT 7:15.notes: Ezek 22:27 LXX princes-of-Israel-as-wolves-tearing-prey (λύκοι ἁρπάζοντες verbatim-match for ἅρπαξ); Jer 23:16 LXX warns against false-prophets' visions-from-own-heart; Deut 13:1-5 LXX Torah-test-of-prophets.

MAT 7:16.notes: Isa 5:1-7 LXX vineyard-expected-grapes-yielded-wild-grapes = prototype fruits-reveal-true-nature. Ps 1:3 LXX righteous-as-fruit-bearing-tree. Jdg 9:8-15 LXX Jotham's-fable thornbush-bramble-not-fruit-tree provides classical thornbush-can't-bear-fruit trope.

MAT 7:19.notes: Near-verbatim self-echo of MAT 3:10 (John-the-Baptist's warning) — only omits οὖν. Matthew deliberately aligns Jesus's Sermon-conclusion with John's warning-formula, showing teaching-continuity. Isa 10:33-34 LXX Assyrian-branches-lopped-off and Ezek 31 LXX cedar-of-Lebanon-cut-down underlie the arboreal-judgment imagery.

MAT 7:21.notes: Mal 1:6 LXX 'if I be Father, where is my honor?' — question of profession-without-honor. Hos 6:6 LXX 'I-desire-mercy-not-sacrifice, knowledge-of-God-more-than-burnt-offerings' underscores deed-over-profession; Matt will cite Hos 6:6 explicitly at 9:13, 12:7.

MAT 7:22.notes: 'That-day' is Day-of-Lord eschatological-idiom: Amos 5:18 LXX ἡ ἡμέρα τοῦ κυρίου, Joel 2:31 LXX, Mal 4:1-5 LXX (Mal 3:19-24 LXX) — day of judgment. Jer 14:14 LXX + Jer 27:15 LXX (Jer 34:15 LXX) rebuke false-prophets who 'prophesy-in-my-name' without divine-commission — exact-scenario Jesus describes.

MAT 7:23.notes: DIRECT CITATION of Ps 6:8 LXX (Ψ 6:9 LXX): ἀπόστητε ἀπ᾽ ἐμοῦ πάντες οἱ ἐργαζόμενοι τὴν ἀνομίαν. Matthew substitutes synonym ἀποχωρεῖτε for ἀπόστητε but preserves οἱ ἐργαζόμενοι τὴν ἀνομίαν verbatim-from-LXX. In Psa 6 David-as-righteous-sufferer dismisses enemies; Jesus takes the Divine-Judge's voice and speaks the Psalm directly — major Christological claim that Jesus occupies position-of-YHWH-Judge.

MAT 7:24.notes: Isa 28:16 LXX foundation-stone-in-Zion λίθον ἀκρογωνιαῖον — messianic-foundation-image; Jesus recalls πέτρα-foundation here, using same word at Matt 16:18 for Peter/Church. Prov 10:25 LXX 'when-the-storm-passes, the-wicked-is-no-more, but-the-righteous-has-eternal-foundation' — wisdom-parallel for enduring-righteous vs collapsing-wicked.

MAT 7:25.notes: Ezek 13:10-14 LXX false-prophets-whitewash untempered-wall-collapses-in-storm — prototype of 'built-on-falsehood collapses-when-tested.' Prov 12:7 LXX 'house-of-wicked-will-be-overthrown, but-dwelling-of-righteous-will-stand.'

MAT 7:27.notes: Climactic collapse-imagery echoes Prov 12:7 LXX (wicked-house-overthrown) and Isa 28:17 LXX ('hail-will-sweep-away refuge-of-lies, waters-will-overflow hiding-place'). Matthean-Sermon concludes with OT-wisdom-eschatological judgment-imagery.

MAT 8:2.notes: Leper's approach to Jesus presupposes the Lev 13-14 leprosy purity code. The term καθαρίζω ('make clean') has both ceremonial (Lev 14 ritual purification) and therapeutic (heal the disease) senses — both are at play in the leper's request. Matthean framing shows Jesus operating within the Mosaic purity framework but with messianic authority to declare clean.

MAT 8:4.notes: Jesus directs the healed leper to follow the Mosaic purification law: priest-inspection + prescribed offering (Lev 14:1-32 detailed protocol with two male lambs, a ewe lamb, grain offering, oil). Matthean theme that Jesus fulfills rather than abolishes the law (cf. Matt 5:17). The priest's inspection would publicly validate the miracle and serve as a Messianic sign (εἰς μαρτύριον αὐτοῖς).

MAT 8:10.notes: Jesus's marveling at Gentile faith and contrasting with Israel's faith-deficit presupposes the Abrahamic promise of blessing to all nations (Gen 12:3, 22:18). Centurion functions as exemplar of proto-fulfillment. Sets up v.11 which explicitly develops the motif with patriarchs + Gentile-banquet imagery.

MAT 8:11.notes: Composite allusion to the messianic-banquet tradition. ISA 25:6-9 LXX describes YHWH's eschatological feast on Mount Zion for 'all peoples' (πάντα τὰ ἔθνη) with wine and rich food. PSA 107:3 LXX gathers the redeemed 'from east and west and north and south' (ἀπὸ ἀνατολῶν καὶ δυσμῶν καὶ βορρᾶ καὶ θαλάσσης) — Matthew's ἀπὸ ἀνατολῶν καὶ δυσμῶν is verbatim from Psalm. GEN 12:3 undergirds the inclusion-of-Gentiles logic. Second Temple parallels: 1 Enoch 62:14; 2 Baruch 29:4.

MAT 8:12.notes: Βρυγμὸς τῶν ὀδόντων echoes LXX PSA 112:10 (Heb 111:10): τοὺς ὀδόντας αὐτοῦ βρύξει καὶ τακήσεται ('he will gnash his teeth and melt away') — wicked gnash teeth at their judgment. Matthew's signature 'weeping and gnashing of teeth' formula (6x: 8:12, 13:42, 13:50, 22:13, 24:51, 25:30) develops this OT trope. Additional backgrounds: Isa 30:33 (Tophet/burning place); Isa 65:13-14 (servants feasting / others crying out).

MAT 8:17.notes: DIRECT OT CITATION, Matthean fulfillment formula (ὅπως πληρωθῇ). LXX Isa 53:4 reads οὗτος τὰς ἁμαρτίας ἡμῶν φέρει καὶ περὶ ἡμῶν ὀδυνᾶται ('this one bears our SINS and is in pain for us'). Matthew's Greek = αὐτὸς τὰς ἀσθενείας ἡμῶν ἔλαβεν καὶ τὰς νόσους ἐβάστασεν ('he took up our SICKNESSES and carried our DISEASES') — matches Hebrew MT חֳלָיֵנוּ הוּא נָשָׂא וּמַכְאֹבֵינוּ סְבָלָם much more than LXX. Matthew uses his own translation from Hebrew (or a non-LXX Greek version) specifically because LXX's 'sins' would not fit the physical-healing context. INTERPRETIVE MOVE: Matthew applies Isa 53 servant-bearing motif to Jesus's healing ministry, expanding the atonement-application (which 1 Pet 2:24 uses for the cross). Matthew sees the cross and the healings as two expressions of the same bearing-for-us reality.

MAT 8:20.notes: First 'Son of Man' (ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου) self-designation in Matthew. Dan 7:13-14 LXX/Theodotion describes the υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου figure who approaches the Ancient of Days and receives dominion and glory. Matthean use exploits the title's double aspect: (1) generic 'human one' (as in Ezekiel), (2) specific figure receiving eschatological dominion. Paradoxical inversion: the one destined for cosmic dominion has nowhere to lay his head.

MAT 8:21.notes: Disciple's request to bury his father presupposes the Jewish sacred duty to bury kin. Lev 21:1-3 allows even priests (who are normally ritually off-limits from corpses) to become unclean to bury immediate family. The 5th-commandment 'honor father' was understood to include burial. Jesus's reply in v.22 therefore places kingdom-loyalty above the most sacred family duty in the Torah — a shocking prioritization.

MAT 8:22.notes: Continues the burial-duty framework from v.21. Jesus's 'let the dead bury their own dead' either (1) uses νεκρός in a spiritual sense ('let the spiritually-dead handle their physical-dead') or (2) is a hyperbolic reductio ad absurdum showing that kingdom-call supersedes every other obligation including the most sacred. Either way, the Torah burial duty is subordinated to discipleship — a Matthean intensification of Jesus's radical call.

MAT 8:24.notes: Jesus sleeping during sea-storm echoes Jonah 1:4-6 LXX. Both are asleep in a boat during a violent storm that threatens to destroy the ship. INVERSE TYPOLOGY: Jonah is the cause of the storm (fleeing God's call); Jesus is the stiller of the storm. The disciples-ask-prophet-to-pray motif (Jon 1:6 'arise, call on your God!') is inverted to disciples-cry-to-Jesus-for-salvation (Matt 8:25). Matthew's σεισμός (earthquake-word) choice over Mark's λαῖλαψ deepens Matthean cosmic-disturbance motif (cf. Matt 27:54 earthquake at death; 28:2 earthquake at resurrection).

MAT 8:26.notes: PSA 107:28-29 LXX (LXX 106): 'they cried to the Lord in their distress, and he delivered them from their troubles; he made the storm to be still, and the waves of the sea were quiet.' The exact OT template for Jesus's storm-stilling. Additional echoes: PSA 89:9 (God rules the raging sea); PSA 65:7 (God stills the roaring of the seas); Isa 51:10. In OT framework, only YHWH stills seas — Jesus's doing so implicitly claims divine prerogative, which the disciples' v.27 amazement implicitly recognizes.

MAT 8:29.notes: The demons' phrase πρὸ καιροῦ ('before the [appointed] time') presupposes an eschatological judgment timetable. DAN 7:9-10 LXX describes the Ancient of Days opening the books and rendering judgment; JOE 2:31 and OT prophetic literature develop 'the day of the LORD' as appointed judgment. Second Temple apocalyptic (1 Enoch 10) gives evil spirits a fixed timetable of confinement until eschatological judgment. Demons thus demonstrate (1) correct theology of Jesus's identity (υἱὲ τοῦ θεοῦ) and (2) correct eschatology (a set time of judgment) — orthodox doctrine without salvific faith, cf. James 2:19.

MAT 8:30.notes: Pigs (χοῖροι) are unclean animals per LEV 11:7 and DEU 14:8. Their presence in large herds signals Gentile (Greco-Roman Decapolis) territory — Jews would not raise pigs. Background detail that sets up the clean/unclean reversal motif (unclean spirits + unclean animals + unclean Gentile land). ISA 65:4 LXX also mentions swine-flesh eaters as marks of apostasy.

MAT 9:2.notes: PSA 103:3 LXX (102:3): ὁ ἐξιλασκόμενος πάσας τὰς ἀνομίας σου ('who forgives all your iniquities') + ISA 43:25 LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἐξαλείφων τὰς ἀνομίας σου ('I, I am he who blots out your transgressions') — both establish forgiving-of-sins as divine-prerogative. Jesus's pronouncement here implicitly occupies YHWH's position, which the scribes correctly interpret as blasphemy in v.3 — Matthew's point is that the interpretation is correct but the conclusion ('blasphemy') wrong because Jesus IS God's Son with authority to speak as such.

MAT 9:4.notes: Matthew's εἰδώς ('having-known') the scribes' thoughts presupposes the OT theme of divine omniscience. PSA 139:2 LXX (138:2): σὺ ἔγνως τὸν καθεδρασμόν μου καὶ τὴν ἔγερσίν μου, σὺ συνῆκας τοὺς διαλογισμούς μου ('you understand my thoughts from afar'). 1KG 8:39 LXX (Solomon's temple-dedication): σὺ μονώτατος οἶδας τὴν καρδίαν ('you alone know the heart'). 1SA 16:7 LXX: ἄνθρωπος ὄψεται εἰς πρόσωπον, ὁ δὲ θεὸς ὄψεται εἰς καρδίαν ('man sees the outward appearance, God sees the heart'). Jesus's mind-reading is theologically loaded — he exercises what the OT reserves to YHWH.

MAT 9:6.notes: Son of Man (ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου) self-title grounded in Dan 7:13-14 LXX/Theodotion. DAN 7:14: ἐδόθη αὐτῷ ἐξουσία καὶ τιμὴ βασιλική ('authority and kingly honor was given to him'). Jesus's claim to exercise ἐξουσία ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ('authority on earth') for sin-forgiveness extends Dan 7's eschatological-heavenly authority into present-earthly ministry. This is the SECOND Son-of-Man statement in Matthew (after 8:20); here the title is linked explicitly to authority. The sin-forgiving authority of Dan 7's figure is not in Daniel itself — Jesus is making a constructive-theological extension.

MAT 9:13.notes: DIRECT CITATION of Hos 6:6 LXX: διότι ἔλεος θέλω καὶ οὐ θυσίαν, καὶ ἐπίγνωσιν θεοῦ ἢ ὁλοκαυτώματα ('for I desire mercy and not sacrifice, and knowledge of God rather than burnt-offerings'). Matthew quotes only the first half (ἔλεος θέλω καὶ οὐ θυσίαν) — exactly verbatim. Matthean distinctive: this citation appears in Matt 9:13 AND Matt 12:7 but is absent from Mark 2:17 parallel. In Hosea's context, YHWH rebukes Ephraim's superficial sacrifice-piety; Jesus re-applies the rebuke to Pharisaic scrupulosity about table-fellowship. The citation frames Jesus as the prophetic voice of YHWH. Interpretive payload: mercy-toward-sinners > ritual-separation-from-sinners. Hebrew חֶסֶד (ḥesed, covenant-loyalty-love) is rendered ἔλεος in LXX; Matthew inherits this.

MAT 9:15.notes: Jesus's self-application of νυμφίος (bridegroom) presupposes the OT tradition of YHWH as Israel's bridegroom-husband. ISA 54:5 LXX: ὅτι κύριος ὁ ποιήσας σε, κύριος σαβαώθ ὄνομα αὐτῷ ('your Maker is your husband'). ISA 62:5 LXX: ὡς νυμφίος εὐφραίνεται ἐπὶ νύμφῃ ('as the bridegroom rejoices over the bride'). HOS 2:19-20 LXX: μνηστεύσομαί σε ἐμαυτῷ εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα ('I will betroth you to myself forever'). EZK 16:8-14 LXX: elaborate allegory of YHWH finding Israel as a foundling, betrothing, clothing. Jesus's implicit self-claim to the bridegroom role is a claim to divine identity. The ἀπαρθῇ (be-taken-away) passive is Matthew's first veiled passion-prediction — explicit predictions begin 16:21.
